top_profane.text:
This is a comparison of the 36” x 78” Pro Impact Mat with Dick’s Sporting Goods 36” X 77” mat. I ordered the Pro Impact, but misunderstood the delivery date. The result was that I also purchased the slightly shorter (only 1 inch) mat from Dick’s so that I would have it when my treadmill was delivered.
Cost: Very similar—Dick’s was $40 and the 4mm Pro Impact was $46
Aesthetic: Dick’s has an attractive finished look. The Pro Impact has an industrial look.
Smell: Dick’s none. Pro Impact has a clear rubber smell. Some reviewers say this goes away as time passes, but still…
Performance: I have only used Dick’s and it absorbs vibration and seems suitable. I am sure Pro Impact would be just as suitable in this regard.
Conclusion: Because of Dick’s superior finish and lack of smell, I would be inclined to buy it again. Side note: Although many treadmills are 80” in length, the portion that actually touches the floor is closer to 75-76 inches. My Sole F63 treadmill fits perfectly on a 77” mat.
top_profane.title: Two Mats Compared

top_profane.text:
Initially, I thought Costa was a good brand that people just didn't really know about yet and I would still think that, if they had any concept of customer service or actually stood by their product quality. They will use any excuse and anything you say to get out of repairing them under warranty. This is actually my second pair from them...this is my first pair with glass lenses - 580 (BUILT BY HAND - BACKED FOR LIFE! Right!!!).

Two or three days after buying them, I came out to my car for lunch....maximum three hours....I take early lunches. So morning time (in the summer though, but still it is just barely warming up!), not in the middle of the day, and I left the glasses in an open cubby in my car pushed kind of to the back of the cubby to be IN THE SHADE - IN THEIR CASE! I came out, and I noticed a tiny crack in the lens...I was worried. By the time I got back to work (after going through a drive through) the little crack spidered out to well over an inch and increased to several lengthy cracks. Okay, so I got a lemon I thought. These MUST be defective! I go online to figure out how to send them in...and guess what? You have to pay 12 bucks upfront before they will even look at them. Uh...okay?!?! Really?!?! But I think to myself....it will be worth it. These were 300 bucks (which in honesty I didn't have to pay - they were comped at a casino which the Costa repair manager did enjoy throwing in my face as a reason why 90 bucks shouldn't be that big of a deal, but so what? It is the principle of the matter! I guess I should have picked the Ray Bans instead!), anyway...I thought it would be worth it to pay another 12 bucks to have them back. A week or so later I get an email saying that it will be and additional 90 dollars to replace the lenses! Are you kidding me? This is my fault?!?!?! Your crappy product can't take a tiny bit of time in the car in the MORNING, they must be carried with me everywhere?!?!?! No that can't be...and you expect everyone to know that? So I called to complain and got the run around from the agent even after demanding to talk to a supervisor (they were ALL in a meeting!) They told me that it was in the warranty that extreme temperature is an exclusion and I could look at it online. So I did...it wasn't EVEN there! I told them that...and they said OH...right...just a sec...it is in the FAQ!!!! Really, the FAQ!!!!!!! Are you joking?!?!?! You must be... You expect customers after purchasing your crappy product to go to your website and read the FAQ!?!? And I asked what exactly they meant by extreme temperature....response...none. When I hear that I, like other normal people think Antarctica or The Serengeti...not Oklahoma in the morning! They are now sending them back to me NOT repaired. They offered to drop the price to 70 bucks just to get me off the line and I said no. I need pictures for all the online reviews I will be doing of your crappy product. I asked if that was worth 70 dollars to them, no response, so I hung up. I guess it is because I just got an email saying they were being sent back. I don't think it is right for a company to charge that much for sunglasses that should really cost 15 bucks. I expect that from Wal-Mart sunglasses. If they want to charge that much, they really need to be more flexible with their customers on warranty issues. Why should I have to pay more when something is your fault. Because I was honest and tried to tell you what actually happened, you used that information to blame the breakage on me, and then you think you have me in a bind and can charge me another outrageous amount of money. No, no you can't do that! I will NEVER buy another pair of crappy Costas, and I will warn everyone I can, in every way I can, about your crappy products, your outright REFUSAL to back up your crappy products, and your lying, rude customer service people! Can you tell I'm pissed? And I'm not the only one...reviews from fishermen who normally speak highly of Costas....[...]
top_profane.title: Costa-mer Service Sucks!

top_profane.text:
Love it so far! Avg hand size and doesn't seem to large to me. Actually like the palm shelf, it helps to stabilize as much as possible. I thought it was a gimmick at first, but I won't take it off now.
PROS:
*NICE ENERGY! I like the m/s printout it came with, says mine got 192 m/s avg over 5 shots taken, (tight spread too) which is 629 ft per second with a "crosman ultra magnum" which if printout pellet is correct, that's a 10.5gr pellet, which translates into 9.2 foot pounds of energy, or 7fpe if that were a regular 7.9gr crosman premier domed pellet, neither is too shabby for a 3 cocker (I do not believe this listing is accurate when it says 4 power levels in the bullet points, my instructions say 3. The listing contradicts itself, saying 4 in one place and 3 in another) I would not even try 4 cocks with this, 3rd cock is decently stiff but not a turnoff. 4 would be pretty difficult and could damage seals imho.
*I like the trigger. I have not felt the need to adjust it one bit. It ships with the lightest trigger tension as the factory default by the way, and I know when it's going to break and fire, it takes some getting used to like anything else, but it's pretty nice imo. It is NOT a long creepy stiff trigger like some other lower quality airguns I've shot over the years. I can pull this trigger with a slow soft squeeze and follow through continued squeeze like butter, it's not jumpy or strange feeling.
*Great safety too, super easy to flip off safe before firing with a forward flick of your trigger finger.
*Accuracy is quite good AFTER putting about 100+ pellets through it. Do not expect this to shoot awesome right out of the box, it takes some break in shots. I put about 8 different types of pellets through it and mine performs best with RWS H-Point 6.9grains, RWS 8.3 superdomes, RWS meisterkugeln 8.2grain (want to try the lighter 7.0 grain version too) and even the heavy gamo rockets 9+ grainers do pretty well. I have some 7.1 grain H&N hollowpoints (better QC on those than the RWS H-Point btw) and various JSB sample pack on the way to try. The groups have tightened almost magically by themselves after putting 100 some shots through it. At first I was all over the place with everything I tried and not too thrilled given the cost, then voila, groups tightened considerably almost instantly, it was weird, but good weird! My gut says it's going to get even tighter as I go, say around 150-200shots. Currently getting approx 1" groups at 10 yards from a padded rest, stock and butt both have a pad underneath. Freehand I can hit a 2 inch 5 shot group at 10 yards which isn't exactly terrible given I've barely shot it thus far free handed, maybe 20 times that way. Once I find the magic pellet I'm confident that group will shrink.
*All adjustments seem very easy to do, however I don't feel the need to adjust a single thing at this point.
*BEST PART: Almost ZERO recoil/vibration when firing (I believe that and the great grips are what contribute to this guns accuracy the most, other than pellet choice.) If shooting an airgun with a lot of vibration you have to wonder if some of that vibration is affecting pellet before it gets out of the barrel. With this airgun, that effect would be very very minimal.

CONS:
The rear sight is reasonably stout as it is, but I thought it would be even stouter.
The front sight needs a dot when you flip it down to expose the shorter long distance front blade. (cool feature btw) The higher blade has a red dot, why not put a white dot on the smaller one? I wish higher sight had a white dot instead of red, my eyes see a white dot 10x better than dark red. I would put white dots on the rear sight as well instead of all black.
Those are tiny nitpick cons, I can find no flaws that would make me hesitant to recommend this to anyone.

I really like this airgun! I was going to go the pcp route but couldn't justify the cost only to get decreasing pellet velocity shot after shot and I can get pretty good velocity out of this at 3 cocks. 2 cocks is decent for practice btw. I shot some at 1 cock and it's super quiet with 1 cock, 3 cocks isn't so loud that I thought my neighbors would mind, it wasn't that sharp of a pop like some others I've shot.

All in all, pretty cool and accurate airgun with a nice trigger/grips, and pretty impressive velocity for only 3 pumps.
top_profane.title: Lot's of Pros, very few Cons... read on

top_profane.text:
I gave three stars since the lantern unit itself seems to be what I expected for this price point. The batteries I received however, were just garbage.

I actually purchased three of these units (1-SUBOOS Ultimate Rechargeable LED Lantern, and 2-SUBOOS Gen 2 ULTIMATE Rechargeable Camping Tent Lantern) with each of the three units claiming to supply 2 2600 Mah 18650 batteries. I have a BT-C3400 Universal Battery Charger Analyzer Tester which I used to test the batteries and was expecting the batteries to be somewhat less than advertised (as most are), however I did not expect the absolutely dismal results that the testing revealed.

Note: I do understand that these Li-ion batteries can require a few charge/discharge cycles prior to producing their best output, so I ran each of them through multiple refresh (charge/discharge) cycles and the results posted below are the highest output of any of the cycles!

The capacity test results on the six batteries I received...

2234 Mah (86% of advertised capacity) - Acceptable
1982 Mah (76% of advertised capacity) - Borderline acceptable
1513 Mah (61% of advertised capacity) - very limited - mostly crap
1099 Mah (42% of advertised capacity) - Pure Crap - Throw in garbage
971 Mah (37% of advertised capacity) - Pure Crap - Throw in garbage
921 Mah (35% of advertised capacity) - Pure Crap - Throw in garbage

In reading other reviews it appears that others have had better results with the batteries they received, so I may have received a bad batch of batteries or they may have just switched to an inferior supplier for their batteries.

Bottom line, if you purchase these lanterns based upon my test results you may want to order some decent batteries to replace the pure crap batteries that you may receive with the unit.
top_profane.title: Lantern OK, Supplied batteries are garbage.

top_profane.text:
Fits my Tippmann Custom 98 without modifications, but I actually prefer the more flush appearance of the original Rocket Cock.

This product allows you to use the cocking pin that comes with the marker. This makes it a little easier to fit on to my BT-4 (note that doing this modification will likely void the warranty). You do have to modify the rocket cock II a little with a Dremel to sand down the actual Rocket Cock II and to drill a hole into the top part of the grip on the marker to access the screw-hole on the aluminum body of the marker itself. Not an absolutely perfect fit, but pretty darn close. Certainly can't tell unless you look at the marker carefully. Functions very well, and protects the cock slot. Best way to cover the cock slot, at least until BT comes with its own version of the Rocket Cock.
top_profane.title: Fits BT-4 with modifications

top_profane.text:
Let's get the big controversy out of the way first: The trailer as a whole is great but as others have reported the tires that come with it are junk. But it's so inexpensive - I paid a few pennies more than eighty-four dollars here on Amazon - I didn't hold the tires against it. Since I read the reviews before making my purchase, I expected the tires to be low quality Chinese junk. I don't know if the tubes are equally crappy because I haven't yet needed to remove the tires to look at the tubes. I haven't had any trouble with them yet but if I have flats or blowouts I've got the right size tubes in my spare parts box already since the front wheel of my recumbent uses the same size tubes (16"/305 x 1.75"). If I ever take the trailer any real distance I'll bring spare tubes for it just as I bring spare tubes for my bike on such a trip. And while the tires are junk they aren't going to be holding the weight of an adult, only a max of 70lbs and usually a lot less, so I don't think they'll be a problem. If the tires do prove to be a problem there are a bunch of 16"/305 tires in my local bike shop and here on Amazon, some relatively inexpensive but plenty good enough (such as Kenda Kwests) and others a bit more expensive but really, really good (such as Schwalbe Big Apples or Marathon GGs). For now I'll use the included tires and tubes until they wear out or cause a problem; I am prepared for but don't expect a problem and they should last a year or more before needing replacement.

I am moving along an asymptote toward a nearly car-free lifestyle. I do most of my local travel, errands, and visiting using a bike; mostly my Sun EZ-1 Lite recumbent although I also have an early-90s Schwinn hybrid and a little Dahon folder. The one thing I found myself still wanting a car for is to do shopping trips that involve bulk items such as paper towels, larger potted plants, etc. The solution to this is a cargo trailer. There are lots available and I did my research, eventually settling on the Burley Travoy. Unfortunately, there is no good way to get the Travoy's hitch to work with my EZ-1 because it has no seat tube and the rack is too low to the ground; too bad because I really liked the ability to use the Travoy cargo trailer as a handcart. After spending over a month trying to come up with inventive ways to get the Travoy hitched to my EZ-1, I went back to research mode and narrowed my choices down to the Burley Nomad and the Allen Sports ACT200 Explorer. The Nomad is a very high-quality trailer from a company well-known for its high-quality products, with more interior cargo space and a higher weight capacity than the Explorer. The Nomad is also lighter because it has an aluminum frame vs the Explorer's steel frame and a much better-designed hitch using an elastomer vs the Explorer's universal joint. But the Explorer had two things going for it that I liked a lot: It could be quickly converted to a convenient handcart mode and it was way, way, WAY less expensive than the Nomad. Even at the highest prices I saw for the Explorer (around one-hundred twenty five) and the lowest I saw for the Nomad (around two-hundred thirty) there was a one-hundred dollar difference - you can pretty much buy two or three Explorers for the price of one Nomad. Even if the reviews claiming that the Explorer's included tires and tubes were so bad they would need to be immediately replaced were true, the Explorer remained a bargain. So I decided to at least give the Explorer a try since the price was so appealing and it had that handcart mode.

The Explorer cargo trailer comes almost fully assembled in its box. Just lift it out of the box, unzip the zipper, pull the wheels out of the cargo compartment, pop the wheels on, then attach the hitch arm to the trailer. This takes barely longer to do than it does to read about. After that, attach the hitch plate to your bike's rear axle. I purchased two extra hitch plates directly from Allen Sports (they're not available here on Amazon) in order to have a hitch plate on all three of my bikes. This is also trivially easy to do, although it's possible with a quick-release rear hub that you'll find that the hitch plate is thick enough so that the skewer won't be long enough with the hitch plate attached. If that's the case, go to your local bike shop or look around here on Amazon or another online store for a longer skewer; they're not expensive and they're easy to find. The hitch plate fit fine on my Schwinn (quick-release skewer) and Dahon (nutted axle) but not on my EZ-1 (quick-release skewer) but I had a longer skewer for the EZ-1 sitting in my spare parts box so that didn't slow me down.

I did experience a moment of confusion when attaching the wheels. At first, I thought that the wheel axles were supposed to go under the trailer bed, partially supporting the trailer bed. But the first time I put a load into the trailer bed, the trailer bed sagged down and the axles poked into the sides of the trailer. I communicated via email with Allen Sports and they told me that the axles are SUPPOSED to poke into the sides of the trailer and in fact the cargo compartment is reinforced in that area to prevent damage. This is not apparent in any of the pictures or videos I saw before my purchase but you'll see the reinforced patches right away as you pull the trailer out of its box; I thought they were just decorative at first.

Before putting the wheels on, pressurize them. The tires on the wheels are rated for 30-60PSI but there is a stick-on label on the wheels that says "30 PSI MAX" so pump them to 30PSI, no higher. If you don't have a pump with a pressure gauge, get one. If you over pressurize and the crappy tubes pop the crappy tires off the rims or the crappy tubes =POP= that's probably your fault, not the fault of Allen Sports or Amazon. By the way, the wheels are mostly plastic but they seem to be perfectly round and straight; weird if you're used to "normal" bike wheels with separate hub, spokes, and rim but they seem to work fine and certainly there should be no need for truing these wheels or using rim tape to protect the inner tube from the nonexistent spokes.

The universal joint at the end of the hitch arm which attaches to the hitch plate on the bike works fine. It's a good inexpensive way to allow the bike to move in relation to the trailer without damaging the hitch arm, trailer, or bike. The trailer can flip over without affecting your bike (other than the drag if you keep riding, of course), or you can lay your bike down on the ground without affecting the trailer. An elastomer would be better as a universal joint can get stiff or even experience damage if movement occurs around more than one axis simultaneously; but of course the universal joint is much less expensive and that's what you get with the Explorer: An inexpensive solution that works.

I don't notice the empty trailer behind my bike at all, except for the noise of it bouncing around on the potholes. The first couple of times I used it on smooth roads I had to keep looking back to be sure it was still there; when empty, it doesn't affect the handling of the bike at all and even though it's 20lbs empty I didn't notice it even on uphills. Loaded with 60lbs of soil from the garden store, sure, I noticed it going uphill but on the flats or downhills it was like it wasn't there except when starting or stopping. Of course it's harder to ride uphill pulling more weight and it takes a little more effort to accelerate and you have to give yourself more room to brake to a stop from a high speed. Physics, DUH! The trailer itself, however, rides better with a load than without because at 20lbs empty it bounces around on rough road quite a bit but even a modest load, another 20lbs or so, helps to keep it from bouncing around. Low-pressure tires designed for shock absorption, such as the Schwalbe Big Apple, would probably reduce the bouncing while unloaded.

The zippered compartment, when zipped closed, is 26" long/19" wide/7" high which looks and functions larger than it sounds. It's good for small items and pizzas. For most shopping, the zippered compartment works nicely and holds quite a bit of stuff, more than the two baskets on my Schwinn combined. It's also good for keeping things inside clean (freshly washed laundry, for example) and dry (the cargo compartment isn't waterproof but if you get caught in a sprinkle for a short time it's better than nothing). The cargo compartment also has an interior divider that can be zipped up or folded flat, leaving a slightly larger space in the rear and a slightly smaller space in the front. It's hard to get stuff into or out of the rear space when the divider is zipped up because the top cover gets in the way. It might be useful for people carrying only a few items, or if you want to be able to reach some items easily while keeping other stuff covered. But I haven't used the divider yet, I just keep it unzipped and folded flat, leaving one large space in the cargo compartment. I often find myself unzipping the top, laying it mostly flat on the trailer bed, and then putting bulky items on top of that. The interior side panels have three tie-down ring anchors each and I keep three long bungies and a cargo net to use with them; the cargo net for multiple smaller items and the bungies for big items like a box I have to bring to the post office.

One of the things I like most about the Explorer is handcart mode. To go from trailer mode to handcart mode, just detach the hitch arm from your bike, unscrew a big black knob on the trailer, swivel the hitch arm from horizontal to vertical, then screw down that big black knob to tighten the hitch arm in that position. It's great for shopping because you fill it up as you walk around the store, unload it at checkout, then load it back up again after paying with no need to waste plastic or paper bags. No need to compete with other shoppers for a cart because you have your own and no worries that you're buying too much stuff to bring home in a single trip. It's not quite as useful or convenient to use this way as a Travoy, but at 1/2-1/3 the price of a Travoy the Explorer is plenty good enough.

To repeat the point of the first paragraph of this review: Please note that my 5-star rating is not a comment on the quality of the tires and tubes. I've seen the tires, I agree with other reviews that they are crap. I haven't seen the tubes (because they're inside the tires and I haven't yet needed to remove the tires) yet I have no doubt that they're crap, too. But I haven't had issues with them and I have spare tubes on hand if needed, and replacement tires are easy to get if and when needed. The welds on the steel parts as well as all the other bits of the trailer don't have the quality one would get from Burley but everything is functional and strong. Given the price, this trailer is an excellent value. You can pay more (a LOT more) and get better quality. You can't pay less; I don't think there is even a half-decent cargo trailer made by anyone available for a lower price. The handcart mode is invaluable for me and the standard axle hitch attachment makes it usable with any bike, including my EZ-1. I would recommend it for anyone looking for a bargain cargo trailer and for whom the 70lb weight capacity is sufficient.

edit 2015-01-17: I've been using the trailer frequently for a couple of months now and I remain very pleased with it. However, note my comment above about the universal joint. Last night while bringing home pizza for the family, the joint locked up and the trailer flipped onto its side while I was making a turn. I had to disconnect the hitch and "work it" with my hands to loosen it up, after which I reattached it and completed the ride home without incident. I have a feeling this will happen every now and then. Infrequently, but it will happen. Universal joints usually work fine, but they can lock up as mine did last night. While I'm not deducting a star, it would be better if Allen Sports replaced the universal joint with an elastomer or even a metal spring.
top_profane.title: Quality of some parts fairly low but this is a great value for a new cargo trailer.

top_profane.text:
I'm going to this review without a set rating in mind until the very end and then mark the appropriate star when I get there. The reason being that I have such a love and hate relationship with this bag that I don't if I want to return it or just keep the darn thing and buy a 2nd bag to go with it.

First off, the color selection is god awful. You get to pick between some weird brown/red cross color or a depressing gray. I'm not too picky on colors, but damn, that is a weird selection to choose from. Heads up to Osprey: "make every model of bag available in black at the very least." I did pick the gray, in case you were wondering. I am going to minus half a star though for the utter lack of decent color choices. So that puts us at 4.5 stars.

Secondly, the smaller zipper pocket has 2 compartments, there's the fragiles pocket lined with softer material, the Ulock/stretchy pocket, 2 water bottle/canteen pockets, the main compartmetn with 2 weird skinny yellow pockets that are as deep as the bag itself, the cover pocket, and the water bladder pocket. With that being said, there really isn't much for organization if you think about it, with exception to the smaller zipper compartment with 2 pockets. I guess the 2 hip pockets are cool, but I really like having more organization with the larger pockets and this bag is really lacking in the department. The hip pockets are also way too small for any phone made in the last 5 years. The straps could totally have used a pocket at chest level as well. I taking a full star, putting this bag at 3.5 stars now.

The air vent against my back is the greatest freakin backpack invention in the history of backpacks. It has literally been the sole reason why I REALLY do not want to get rid of this bag. I am NOT a pannier type of guy but I absolutely hate having a sweaty back from riding with a backpack on. Not only does my back not sweat (nearly as much, anyways), but I can actually feel a breeze as I get down into the drops and pick up my speed. This puts this bag back at 4.5 stars.

The bag is light. Normally this would be a good thing, but I think it is unnecessarily light. The waterproof cover is of light enough material that the entire bag should have been made from it with the waterproofing used in the zipper pocket for glasses. This would have made the entire bag waterproof and friggin awesome. It's not really that great big of deal, but semi-related is the fact that the damn waterproof cover covers my tail light that I attach to the bag. Why is there no tail light attachment on the damn cover?! Minus half a star, putting it at 4 stars.

The writing on the straps as well as the graphic on the back is peeling and already coming off. I don't care, really, but I've had this bag for a month. Seriosly, I expect this sort of thing after at least a year, from cheap crappy things. Even my 12 year old jansport bag has every single logo readable after all this time. I won't even minus stars, I just had to mention it.

The loose straps are annoying. They are freakin everywhere! They're just flopping around and a real pain in the arse! Minus 1 star! BUT.... They have an amazing purpose and they are extremely convenient for using to adjust to get the exact sizing and compression so, I gotta give the star back! We're still at 4 stars!

But wait, there's more. The curvature of the bag is a real annoyance. Mix this with the lack of a laptop sleeve and this bag is just kind of useless as an urban computer/weekend adventurer hybrid. I have stuffed my laptop into the water bladder pocket (leaving the bladder at home) as a work around but in doing so, I have ripped the cheaper material within the bag because there was a leatherman tool in another pocket and the laptop scraped up against it. Not very durable for sure! I have to minus 1.5 stars for this, putting it at 3 stars!

I've owned this bag for less than a month and I have thoroughly used the crap out of it. I only ride to work, to the gym, and to the store. I haven't even brought it with me on any long trekking/hiking adventurers yet, and that would be that hardest I would think I'd put this bag through. In less than a month that I've had this bag, I've already stretched out the mesh pocket in the main compartment from my laptop chord (even frayed the material), I've stretched the lining material out in the stuff-it pocket (which is also showing discoloration already) from storing my bike/work shoes, I've utterly destroyed the graphic/logos everywhere other than the main black logo, I've warped the crap out of the main straps, and I've already misaligned the bag from the wire frame. Some would say I've simply broken it in, I say I have killed half of this bag's life expectancy and I have to take away another star, leaving it at 2 stars.

In conclusion, this bag feels like it was made up with a bunch of great ideas in mind but it wasn't thoroughly thought through. For instance, there's a bike helmet attachment thingy that's simply a loop. Why?! Why can't wouldn't I just attach it to any of the other 1,000 straps on the darn bag? Why not put the retracting/elastic helmet holder thingy you have on all the other bike bags? Also, there's a tail light attachment area, but not one on the cover, what the %#@! And then there's the hip pockets, a perfect place for a phone... if my phone was the size of gum pack. And the pocket for glasses/electronics is way too big! It totally messes with storing things properly in the main compartment. Things like this just make this bag seem like it COULD have been a great thing. Unfortunately though, I am really REALLY close to returning it. I can't recommend this bag to anyone and for that I have to take away yet another star, putting it at 1 star.

But that "Airspeed" backing though... it's so freakin' awesome man.... damn... have another star back!

** EDIT ** I forgot to add that no matter how you pack this bag, there's no way that it will stand on its own. This annoys the crap out of me. Just another heads up.
top_profane.title: Mixed emotions

Advanced notice: This review will be long and as comprehensive and detailed as I can be with updates as time goes on. If you like short reviews it's 5 stars and skip to the bottom for a summary. If you like details about another perspective and experience read on...

****Update 08-08-2018***
I just got off the phone with Primary Arms tech guy and after asking questions the helpful gentleman looked up the information for me.

To paraphrase the phone call this scope will work with Mk262 from a 16-18" barrel zero'd at 200 yards or 2" high at 100 yards.
It will also work for M80 ball as well as up to 180 grain I forget the bullet and velocity now but iirc it was pretty standard.
Inside the manual for people who don't have an issue reading (bifocals mess with my eyes to much) it sounded like it should work with pretty much every common load for both calibers, but consult the manual yourself by downloading it before purchasing to verify.

So with this information in hand I am incredibly pleased so far and am looking forward to trying to get to the range this evening to revisit some things I mentioned in my original review. I now have my eye relief set as close as I'm able to on my 10/22 and if able to go will be taking a .223Wylde chamber as well!
Yay me!!!!

Also the more I handle this scope it seems like it may well be just about ideal for a DMR optic! Reticle is bold enough to easily see yet not too thick for fairly precise accurate shots. It's not a benchrest scope by any means but a real world user friendly reticle that should work under 98% of all situations needed. Can't wait to try it on deer across a corn field this fall!!!

Yes I'm hopeful and excited about/for this scope and its potential!!!

***End up update****

Original review:

08/02/2018
I received my Primary Arms 4-14X scope today and threw it in a pair of UTG medium rings on top of my semi custom Ruger 1022 just for evaluation purposes. My rifle isn't anything special just a stock Ruger I've had for over 20 years that I finally swapped out the barrel and stock on. Tactical Solutions 16.5" threaded barrel with iron sights and TS muzzle brake, and a Hogue over moulded stock. Nothing too fancy just simple and accurate.

I wasn't able to get to the range until about 7:30-8:00PM so my evaluation was not conducted during optimal light hours. This is relevant especially to a person like myself who has failing eyesight due to a genetic disease that affects my retinas. Translation besides losing my eyesight I can't see at night and have an extremely difficult time in the fading light where most of you see just fine. I almost would be better off to close my eyes or get out a flashlight like it was pitch black outside. I mention and disclose this not for pity or anything other than to illustrate my unique perspective in an attempt to make it easier for you dear reader to get a feel for where my reviews come from. For what it's worth I just can't see at night and do just fine during the day so I'm not "blind" or anything yet but we'll see what I am in another 20 years.

My evaluation was conducted at 50 yards using Remington Thunderbolts and Federal bulk. No Target ammunition because I'm in the process of teaching my kids to shoot and they're not ready for the good stuff yet. They need a little more improvement with the old Remington 514 single shot first before they graduate to scopes etc. Besides that any rifle I've ever had that shot well always did so with Thunderbolts as well as match at 50 & 100 yards.

Targets used were unconventional for accuracy testing however I find them quite suitable for this as well as the fun factor. Clay pigeons and after breaking them on the berm clean up the little pieces with the rifle. Fun for myself and the kids with increasingly smaller targets to shoot with instant visual gratification. So while no measurable accuracy or box tests a fair and fun test of practical accuracy before sandbags and paper.

If you're familiar with my reviews you'll know that I own a variety of optics from Schmidt & Bender to Unertl to Kahles, Hensoldt, Zeiss, Denver Colorado Redfield, Leupold & new Redfield, older Bushnell XXXX Elites, blah blah blah. Enough quality both affordable and not so affordable for most people. I mention this not for penis envy simply like my eyesight to be able to qualify and quantify my opinions. If I feel it's as good or better than X brand Y model then it probably is because I can compare the two should I choose to.

Initial impressions were positive. This is not my first Primary Arms scope so I had a passing familiarity in my expectations. As others have said it's a tank! Well maybe not a tank but it's no lightweight cheap feeling scope. It feels stout and durable.

Turrets moved nicely with crisp clicks I can feel as well as hear. No mush what so ever!

Magnification ring is a little stiff for my likes but it's not unreasonable or unusable. I feel I'll be able to change magnification with winter gloves on just fine. I think firm yet smooth would be an accurate statement regarding it's movement.

I'm drawing a brain fart on the proper terminology however adjusting the ocular end to clean up the blur for my eyesight performed well. I feel that it could've been a little finer however it definitely was not coarse and too abrupt for me. It adjusted quickly and I was able to easily fine tune it for my peepers without feeling like it took all day. So maybe it's just about ideal after all? I'll have to revisit this on my next range trip.

Reticle is perfectly executed, clean, clear etching with no burrs or fuzz. Quite usable on 4X for my failing eyesight although ranging might take me a little more work on 4X than on 10X but while small on 4 it's certainly usable. I'm quite pleased with the reticle and have zero complaints!!!

Illumination is going to be another that I have to revisit simply because I didn't use it at the range. I only checked it out at night after getting home and I can say that 6 appears quite bright for my eyes and 1 while visible at night is likely to be nonexistent during the day. I generally don't use illumination during the day and only what's absolutely needed as light fades. So for now I'm pleased with the illumination.

Slop, wobble, tightness of tolerances or lack there of. Everything I turned, shook, wiggled, etc felt well executed and machined. There is zero wiggle to anything. Threads feel clean and clear, no burrs felt, everything fit tight and really just feels like QUALITY! Movement of things that are supposed to move was smooth as a well groomed woman and like a well groomed woman I have no complaints with either!!!

Eye relief.... Oh the bane of entry level optics!!! This I am going to have to revisit as I need to reposition my rings to get better placement of the scope on the rail. However I felt that from 4-10 eye relief was good. It wasn't super particular however at 11-14 it got a little bitchy and again the scope was not properly positioned so this needs to be revisited. Due to not getting to the range early enough I just threw it all together and did not take the time I should have during set up. So no issue with the scope yet.
I will say that due to the scopes design there is not a lot of room for sliding it around in the rings. Something to be aware of if you have a short rail like a 1022 for example. Likely a non issue in a proper one piece mount versus using super wide rings with six screws each!

Glass quality and clarity... Here's where most entry level optics fail miserably as well and I will revisit this topic as time goes on and I have more experience with this scope. Initially however I want to liken it's clarity to my Zeiss conquest. I'm pretty f-ing pleased there!!! It may be better, it may be worse, but I think that's a good fair starting point for comparison.
My last shots were fired at ~8:30pm and I had no real problems finding the clays or their pieces even though I about went ass over tea kettle on a bench on my way back to the truck. My kid just laughed after making sure I was ok. You can laugh cry or get pissed off in life and I'd rather laugh in a good way because the rest just isn't constructive or helpful so no my kid isn't a dick that's just how we deal with my failing sight.

The boy loved the scope and said to him it was clear and bright up to the end. When asked if he could see better thru it than with the naked eye his reply was basically "Oh yes!!" So the scope does gather light very well and doesn't fail in that fading light most other lower priced optics do.

Of interest to note on 4X clays are boringly easy to break. The horseshoe brackets the clay almost perfectly so it's idiot proof. If you missed a whole clay you really screwed up!!
Also I have to note that I was able to easily clear up the scope to where I could see the ridges on the clay at 50 on 4X.

One thing I have to mention is that I practically ran out of elevation adjustment. IIRC correctly I'm 4 clicks from bottoming out. It occurs to me that I don't recall if the rail I put on the rifle was a 20MOA rail or not and if it was did I put it on backwards? I will revisit this and if I screwed up I will disclose that so you can laugh and shake your head at me to.

I will update this review as time goes on and as I gain more time with this scope. While I'm no bodies fan boy I can't say anything bad really about this scope yet. So in a nutshell...

Sub $300 optic slightly over $300 sitting in new rings.
Extremely clear and bright glass.
Gathers light well. Better than I expected.
Everything that's supposed to move does so smoothly and feels like quality workmanship.
Awesome reticle.
If it continues to perform then it's a solid bargain!!!
Oh my scope did not come with flip up caps but rather one of those rubber bikinis. No points lost here as cheap flip ups suck and bikinis rot so I would've replaced them anyway.

I purchased my scope for full asking price on Amazon receiving no discounts, compensation, or sexual favors for my review. I review everything honestly and try to give the good, bad, ugly, and indifferent in hopes of helping others make educated decisions and to help others out. It is my sincerest hope that this does help people out.
Should you find it helpful please click yes and let Amazon know, however if you find it to be craptastic then by all means click that button as well!

Thanks for reading and again I hope I've helped.
top_profane.title: Initial impressions Awesome scope at an entry level price!!!

Look elsewhere - I will have replaced nearly every part but the frame and I've had it less than three months.

I am a 58yr-old man, 6' tall and weigh about 290 - all muscle, strong as a slightly-chubby, older bull. The Schwinn Discover Hybrid that I bought on December 21, 2018, came with 700c wheels, and it is a bit high for me, but I am able to stand with the middle bar almost touching my boys (about 33" from ground to bar). When first riding, I felt I was forced to lean too far forward and my upper body was quickly fatigued and so I replaced the handlebar with one that let me sit more upright. The front and rear derailleurs both needed multiple adjustments, and they were fine for awhile, but now they need constant adjustments. I am considering replacing the twist shifters with trigger shifters, hoping that will solve this strange problem, but I'll probably end up replacing both derailleurs as well.

Update after 400 miles - the chainring assembly (the front three sprockets) wobbles and I can't find info on how to tighten or replace. It appears to be a problem without a solution and I'm beginning to consider replacing the entire crankset.

The pedals were razor-sharp spiked pedals-from-hell. I replaced the pedals with those old, slippery, non-lethal kind.

PROS: Price at $310 was less than most in this quality range, fairly stable and sturdy overall, spring-loaded fork and seat (but these spring-loaded suspensions are not much of a "pro" - the seat suspension tension adjusts but the forks don't)
CONS: low quality tires, deadly razor-spike pedals-from-hell, crap brakes --- replaced pedals, seat, handlebars and brake pads --- plan on replacing twist shifts with trigger shifts, plan on replacing crap brakes with disc brakes ... but I may end up replace this dumpy bike with something better before I replace all the low-quality parts that this bike came with.

* Seat replacement * this bike comes with a seat that has a specialized assembly bracket at the top of the spring-loaded seat stem, not the standard round shaft adapter, and this required me to remove the assembly components that came with the new seat in order to use the bracket and assembly parts that came with the original seat. Near-standard fit, it seems.

The Promax alloy linear pull brakes, or V-brakes, are difficult to adjust, and the brake contact material is wearing rapidly (though I admit I am a well-muscled person). I've really had it with these crap brakes and am planning on replacing them with disc brakes. The tires are flimsy and both were replaced within a few rides due to getting multiple punctured tubes front and back because the tire thickness allowed tiny objects like plant seeds and small bits of broken glass to puncture the tubes. The seat was too stiff and slender and so I purchased a Cloud9.

The handlebar stem is THREADLESS, not quill, which means it uses neck shims (small, stacked cylinders) to adjust handlebar height and it shipped with the maximum height of 3 shims (about 1" of stem height adjustment). I think the threadless stem system is really stupid because there is no way to simply raise the handlebars several inches, but if you want more height with a threadless system it is my understanding that you must replace the entire neck assembly with a longer one that allows this type of modification. And so there was no way to raise the handlebars enough to allow me to ride in a more comfortable position. I also adjusted the handlebars using the 30 degree rotation of the handlebar stem clamp, but this only added about an inch of height and so I purchased a truly swept-back handlebar from Walmart (through a 3rd party vendor), which also required the purchase of a small handlebar half-cylinder-shim in order to obtain complete tightening of the neck clamp. The handlebar grips that shipped with the bike are very stiff and so I also purchased soft, cushioned grips.

I've been riding the bike almost every day for about six weeks now, and other than replacing the tires, handlebars and pedals, everything has been functioning really, really well. I will probably replace the twist-shifter with a trigger shift-brake handle combination soon, since the twisting is getting annoying, especially the 3 front gears, which require about a full one-inch rotational twisting motion of the left hand, bending the left wrist awkwardly backward, which takes five clicks to switch from first to second on the front gears, but only another two clicks to go to the third, largest front gear. The rear gears are awesome after some adjustment, except for the twisting of course.

I really don't understand why I was unable to find a decent bike with disc brakes in this price range, and the problem with the handlebar neck having almost no height adjustment just blows my mind. The sharp, protruding edges of the original pedals-from-hell seems like they must have been made for people who are always wearing thick, plastic lower-leg protectors for some sport that involves opponents constantly kicking each other in the lower legs, and I feel like those pedals should never be allowed near people for any reason. I have a feeling that these types of dangerous and low-quality parts are being auctioned around among a group of unwanted-parts dealers.

I guess I didn't return this bike because I reasoned that it had been so long (about fifteen years) since I last purchased an inexpensive Giant hybrid, that there were bound to be many significant changes, and most bikes were probably going to require many of these types of modifications. I felt like I would probably have to make many modifications to whatever bike I bought. Can't understand why Giant brand bikes are so expensive now. They're really just "good" quality bikes, not anything incredible.

If I can get the wobble out of the chainring assembly without replacing the entire crankset, then I might keep this Schwinn, otherwise I would consider anything Schwinn makes from here on out to be mostly junk. And shame on Schwinn for putting together a bike full of crappy parts.
top_profane.title: Not "good" - most parts are low quality

I compared 5 anti-fog coatings on my bathroom mirror: Cat Crap, McNett Sea Drops, Fogtech DX MotoSolutions individually packaged wipes, ordinary Suave shampoo, and Shadow Lake Vanilla Almond castile soap.

I washed the mirror with a Windex-like ammonia cleaner, and rubbed the first 3 on the mirror the day before, using a plastic glove for the cat crap, a microfiber cloth for the McNett's, and the wipe itself for Fogtech. The shampoos I added the next morning. After another 6 hours drying, I buffed them all off with a microfiber cloth (being pretty careful not to use the same section of the cloth on different anti-fog coatings. I left areas between the treatments untreated. Then I took a shower. The mirror was on the wall opposite the shower; I don't think any part of the mirror gets more fogged.

All 5 were vastly superior to no treatment at all. I ranked them, from least to most effective:
5. Ordinary shampoo. The clear loser. The glass wasn't cloudy but had a rippled surface of water sticking to it.
4. Cat Crap. It did pretty well, but had some rippling, and some globs of cat crap that became visible only after steaming.
3. Fogtech. It had stripes in the last direction I'd wiped with the wipe. Although that could have been water dripping from the Castile patch above it. Unfortunately I added the shampoo areas above the other 3, which made them not precisely comparable.

First & second place were hard to distinguish; you could call it a tie:
2. McNett's. Worked great. Unfortunately, it's the most toxic, and has warnings not to use in goggles because of that.
1. Castile soap. Only a little better than McNett's if at all. I should add this bottle was probably 15 years old, because I bought it for camping, and I haven't gone camping in a long time.

SECOND DAY: Things changed a bit--I left the mirror alone, and on the second day the ranking was
5. Shampoo
4. McNett's. Got all rippled on day 2.
2. Cat Crap. (Tie)
2. Castile soap.
1. Fogtech.
top_profane.title: Comparison of 5 anti-foggers

top_profane.text:
The wobbler did its job and you can easily change the target's direction, elevation, etc. There is one aspect to consider for you all future buyer--keep in mind this aspect is a matter of physics, not the fault of the product itself.

You see, to cock a target launcher you need to use arm strength to pull the launcher arm (or bar, etc) back against spring tension to cock it for the next launch. As you pull on the aluminum launcher arm, the force of the pull has to be opposed by an equal and opposite force. Recall the law as described by Sir Newton. A STATIONARY setup, i.e., without the wobbler kit, it is actually a lot easier to cock the launcher arm. Simply put, you can pull on the launcher arm with one or both hands (and arms) against the stationary clay thrower that's been bolted down tight. As soon as you install the wobbler kit, the law of physic takes over--simply again: this time when you pull on the launcher, your setup spins around as you try to cock the launcher. Makes sense?

I have to use my left hand to pull on the launcher arm while with my right hand grabbing a hold of the body of the clay thrower trying to stabilize it from spinning around. This makes cocking a whole lot more difficult to accomplish. At times I had to try to use my knee to block the launcher from spinning while I use both and to pull and cock the device. Hmmm.

The problem goes away as soon as the wobbler kit is removed and the whole clay thrower setup is, once again, firmly locked down and immobile. Ah, physics. To every action an equal and opposite reaction is needed to prevent the whole thing from spinning..unless it's bolted down.

So, in conclusion:

1) If the device is to be used in an open field and the loader (the human) has sufficient arm strength to operate the thrower single-armed (the other arm had to stabilize the launcher), this is a perfect addition to the claw thrower.

2) If the device is to be used in a narrow field (say, a shooting range) where there is not a whole lot of angle or elevation that you can play with, a fixed setting is actually adequate and possibly more suitable for the shooting condition.

Thanks for reading!
top_profane.title: Wobbler wobbles, naturally . . .

This seems to be a pretty well designed light. Doesn't change modes when it hits bumps as some I've owned have done. Both flash modes are very good and quite visible in the daytime from a distance.
I think this product could be improved with a waterproof seal (weird they didn't add that since PDW is in very wet Portland)and a snap together body instead of having to use a Phillips to change batteries. Can't guess how it might hold up if it gets caught in heavy rain, but we'll see.
The on/off button shown here is red, but the one on mine is black. The red is a better idea, maybe white (or glow in the dark) would be even better since this is going to be used at night.
Comes with a fender mount, and two sizes of tube mounts. Easy to clip in place and remove from the mounts.
Update Mar 2012: Having used two of these (on my bike and my wifes)for over a year now, I can attest to the usefulness and reliability. No issues, although I have still not been caught in a heavy rain yet with them. We became aware of the availability of different flash patterns when my buddy put two on his commuter bike. My wife also has a Planet Bike Turbo (1 Watt) on her bike. The Radbot seems a bit brighter to me. Also I found the fender mount that came with the Radbot fits the Turbo flash perfectly, in case you have both. We also have found that the Radbot, because of its narrow beam, is also fine for group riding if mounted low on the bike ahead of you.
On pissing off drivers: Because the brightest part of the beam on the Radbot is narrow, it only causes eye discomfort if the cars are failing to move left before they get closer than about 100 feet. If they aren't moving left by then YOU should be pissed. On another note, when I rode with my K2 headlight (about 140 lumens) I used to have a lot of cars approaching in front, turn on their high beams, I guess to see what I was. Really pissed ME off, since it blinded me. But since I got a Magicshine (1000 lumens)they don't do this anymore. I guess they figure I'm a motorcycle. Light is your friend, stop worrying about pissing off jerks, they're already pre-pissed. If someone runs you over on purpose thats called murder. If they run you over because you don't have good lights, thats called you being stupid. Stop thinking of yourself as a victim, stand up for your right to be safe.
top_profane.title: Good visible day light - good for group night rides

top_profane.text: This is my second CAS Hanwei/Tinker sword - I also have the non fullered bastard sword - and this is just as well made as the first. I think the Vikings' mass in a shorter but broader length just has more inertia to overcome. Overall, aesthetically I think this is one of the best looking Viking swords out there and I couldn't be happier.

I have played Racket Ball since my college days and wanted to get my nephews involved in the sport so when this racket set became available, I ordered it. Here is what I found:

1. Is this a Wilson Titanium Racket? No, it is too flexible and will not deliver the power that you can get from a better racket. Is this a good racket? Yes, it is a very well balanced and light racket that I have no trouble playing with. It is just not one that I would prefer. But it is a very good racket to have someone learn with and it certainly beats the heck out of the box store racket sets for quality.

2. The case is kind of a strange fit, but it works and you can actually put two rackets in it if you need to. I would give the case a lifespan of one season of hard use, but it may do better than that. Only time will tell.

.3. The shuttle cocks that come with the set appear to be good quality. You can usually tell a poor quality shuttle cock as they are the ones that become stuck in the webbing of the racket!! Almost always it is a bad shuttle cock rather than the racket when you get it stuck in the racket!!! Spend the money and buy good quality shuttle cocks when these are gone!

4. If you are thinking that because this says set that a net is included, it is NOT. If you think that two rackets are included because it is a set, it is NOT. This set consists of a racket, a tube of shuttle cocks, and a carrying case.

For the money, it is a pretty good set and if you are looking for something for fun or to learn with, this would be a good option. I would recommend it for that.
top_profane.title: A perfect racket for a beginner

top_profane.text:
12/30/12
Purchased at this treadmill at Dicks. It is the 2011 model as far as previous years is concerned. I purchased the $200 4 Year Warranty from Dicks and got everything for under $800 on this bad boy. They were selling off unused floor models and most likely still are. Dicks had a contract with Sole, but now Sole will be sold by sears as far as a retail standpoint goes, so Dicks no longer sells the Sole's at their $1500 price point. As far as the floor model goes, it is under warranty from Sole and Dicks Sporting Goods if I have any problems. It was never used so there was no damage to any parts, just some worn stickers from being in a store.

The details of the treadmill itself is it has a 3.5HP Motor, supports 400 lbs, had speakers, built in programs, and fans. The fans aren't like a high motor fan you would buy from a store, nor should they be. They provide a slight cool breeze and offer air circulation while you are running. Most of the time, you may put a treadmill in a stuffy basement where air doesn't really get pushed around enough so I enjoy this feature unlike some other picky reviews. The 3.5HP motor has a lifetime warranty by Sole. It is a monster and is reliable for running. It can handle high speeds and weights and is better than several other treadmill motors I have run on in the past

The actual treadmill is longer and heavier than your average treadmill. It weighs about 200lbs. If you do move it into a basement, measure the dimensions and print out the manual online because its a really heavy tight fit around corners and stairs. It is more narrow and wide than most treadmills which gives you more running room overall. It comes in 2 pieces and is easy to assemble aside from its weight. I am happy it weighs so much because I like to run at 8 mph and I can come down on the treadmill and it really handles the weight and doesn't shake or budge at all.

This treadmill replaced a Life Fitness treadmill that just had no more bearings to it. The treadmill lost its calibration and became a dud with its maybe 2.0hp motor. The motor on this guy handles speed very well. I can go from 8.0mph to 4.0mph in a matter of seconds. It offers programs for cardio and fat burning. There are shelves for beverages, and whatever else you may need when running.

I highly recommend this treadmill. If you have any comments or questions, please feel free to ask.
