top_rant.text:
At first I was skeptical of the claims and thought it was just another exercise gimmick (remember the shake weight? OMG too funny!). Fifteen lbs in 3 weeks is just not possible unless you're quite a bit overweight (there's 52,500 calories in 15 lbs so be realistic!). I wanted to lose between 10 and 15 lbs but more importantly I wanted to get my fitness routine back on track. Over the years it has devolved into 30 minutes 4 or 5 times a week on the elliptical machine (no weight lifting) and I pretty much ate anything I wanted.

When the package arrived I was a little surprised at how small the containers were (they look larger on TV) but trust me, it's MORE than enough food. I began by reading all the documentation that came with the containers and reviewing the workouts so I would have an idea of what the routines entailed then I got busy organizing my eating plan. You have so many choices that planning a menu from them was easy. I set up a color coded spreadsheet with days of the week outlining the exercise and foods for the day. I made the dressings, spice mixtures, and anything I could ahead of time then portioned and packaged it (snacks too) in microwavable containers. The only thing I left as cook to order was for the evenings where fish was on the menu. It was a quite a bit of work in the beginning but as the weeks progressed, it became much easier and nothing is better after a hard day than coming home to an already prepared HEALTHY yummy dinner! I even set up my spreadsheet so that it would crank out a shopping list from the items plugged into the menu.

I started on a Sunday, taking pics and measurements and I did the Yoga Fix just to get my body familiar with different sorts of movement again. I actually broke a sweat! Next day was Total Body Cardio Fix and it kicked my butt! I could barely get through the warm up without huffing and puffing but by the end of the first week, I had lost 2 lbs so I stuck with it.

By the third day the belly bloat was gone! I looked 10 lbs lighter and I felt like Superman!! By the fourth day, I was actually pushing food away! In the first few weeks, I stuck religiously to the containers, if it fits (and it's on the list) you can eat it but let's face it, one cup of greens does not a salad make so I "cheated" quite a bit with lettuce but not the goodies that go on top. I've never been a fan of oily, goopy salad dressings so all that was needed was one of the dressings from the plan or a sprinkle of vinegar or lemon juice. As the weeks progressed I got sick of salads so I would "cheat" once in a while with a big bowl of pasta or a scoop of ice cream, etc, but never ever cheated by skipping exercise. I was HOOKED!

As to the 3 Day Quick Fix, in a word, blech. Plus, it's super inconvenient. I was having to run home every two hours to heat something up and it just didn't work for me. I gave it a valiant try twice but couldn't get through the three days either time, then I chucked it all together and ate foods from the "regular" menu and exercised once per day instead of the recommended two a days.

Now here I am, 10 weeks later, 16 lbs lighter and 18 inches smaller and I'm still going strong!! My "heavy" weights have become the "light" weights and I can actually do nose/chest to the ground on your toes pushups when before, I couldn't do one, not ONE! It's incredible how fast I progressed and Autumn's friendly, non-drill sergeant style is a nice change from other "programs" I have followed. This one WORKS but you gotta put in the effort. There's no shortcut or magic pill.

Now, the incessant advertising of other Beachbody programs and products between the workouts is annoying but you can fast forward through them. The warning in the beginning releasing them from liability if you're an idiot and try to be Arnold in day takes about a minute so when it's time to change discs, I just let it run while I grab my water, towel and weights cuz you can't fast forward. Once that's past, you'll be right to the main menu and can pick your workout for the day but you'll have to fast forward a vitamin commercial or that blasted Shakeology. I didn't do Shakeology either. I'm skeptical. It seems to me to be too MUCH of a good thing and I have a hard time believing that you get the full benefit as most of what's in there, you'll probably eliminate in the bathroom. Meaning, you'll pee it down the drain as your body won't be able to absorb or get benefit from the ingredients that quickly. Plus, its hella expensive and I have a protein powder I've been using for years as a meal replacement for about a third of the cost that tastes great and keeps me full for hours.

Another criticism would be that there's no age or sex categories for the eating plan until you get to the maintenance stage but even then, it's just separated by men and women as they have slightly different container allotments but still no differentiation for age.

Overall, I would HIGHLY recommend this program regardless of your age or fitness level. I'm female, 48 soon to be 49 and haven't seen the inside of a gym in years. It works!! I have abs again and none of the old lady "bat wings" that were starting to develop. I look forward to my exercise EVERY day when in the past, I was more like, ugh, time to go to the gym. Its fun now! Buy it, live it, lose the weight and feel GREAT!!

***UPDATE***

Ever wonder what happens after the initial success that someone experiences with these programs? I do, so I thought I'd share mine in hopes that the peeps that said my review was helpful would get some benefit or encouragement to start or continue.

Its my 6 month anniversary today and I'm still hard at it but to be honest, I'm a bit bored with the routines (still waiting....patiently...for 21 Day Fix Extreme, c'mon Autumn!!, us "Fixers" need a bigger Fix! Haha.). I never enjoyed the Total Body Cardio/Cardio Fix workouts. I dreaded them in fact. Squat thrusts? Ugh. Hated 'em in 6th grade, double hate them now! LOL. Also, I don't care to hop around with weights which are part of those workouts. So, to combat boredom and still get some cardio I tweaked the program by substituting Shaun T's Dance Party (FUN!! Not like work at all!!) for my cardio days and then I do upper/lower fix, Pilates and Dirty 30! Love Dirty 30!! My favorite by far. I've also taken to giving myself a day off from exercise once per week. I regularly add more weight to keep it challenging and my initial dumbbells are a thing of the past!!. Don't worry ladies, you won't become a muscle head and get "buff" you'll get sleek, sexy and toned! If pressed for time, I pop in the 10 minute fix for abs but never skip a day when exercise is planned cuz I don't feel like it, I'm too tired, or whatever excuse I've used in the past. My weight loss has leveled off now but I've actually lost more than I originally set out to do. More important than the scale, is the way I look and the way my clothes fit. Muffin top? Belly? Back fat? Bat wings? Faaaggghhheetttaabowwwttt it! Haha. I'm FIT and SOLID with DEFINITION (booty included), not skinny and jiggly. If you're still debating, I've had continued success even all these weeks later and I feel better than when I was 25..its completely doable! Its hard to imagine a day without my Fix and I hope anyone that takes it on, enjoys it as much as I have. Its part of me now!! Just keep in mind, you can undo all that hard work in the kitchen. My containers are still my best friend, cuz its sooooo easy to over-portion...especially when there's that last little bit in the yogurt tub or when there's four or five pieces of fruit that won't fit. If it doesn't fit, save it for later or freeze things like extra fruit for smoothies.

Now...go get your fix on! You'll love yourself for it!! Good luck!!

****BREAKING NEWS***** :)

21 Day Fix Extreme has been released! You bet I plunked my money down as soon as it was available! Its on its way now and a quick tracking finds it taking a little rest in Vegas as I write this which means it should be at my door in just a couple short days! Maybe even by tomorrow!! Woot woot! I'll be sure and give my two cents after I've had the time to run through at least one cycle so stay tuned!!

**Final Update**
I just completed the second round of Extreme and as promised, my review is up!
top_rant.title: If you're on the fence, here's my story!!

top_rant.text:
Since there aren't any reviews on this yet let me be the first to break down a few things on this sight.

Background: I wanted an adjustable slider sight for extended range archery because 7-pin sights get WAY too crowded, obscuring the sight picture at distance and you have to remember which is which or else count while your sighting down the target... that's stupid. So I decided on a slider, but the idea of a single pin in a hunting situation didn't appeal that much either because what if that big bull all of a sudden starts ambling over to my rattles and I'm set on a single pin at 70yrds but crap, he's moving in close to 30 now!!! YIKES! So it came down to a multipin slider.... there are a few options out there and I did exhaustive research on each (black gold ascent, CBE, Apex, Spot Hogg and the TR). Each has their pros and cons so maybe I can cover a few for anyone out there who was like me and didn't really know what to look for.

1) Construction: The TR Trio is entirely machined aluminum and it feel premium. It's spend at S$^T but it feels premium, so there's that. My bestie shoots a MBG Ascent 5 pin and it too is built like a tank... really solid. The Trio doesn't feel as solid as that but I went to the shop and looked as a CBE and Axcel and they felt cheaper than the Trio. The housing is really solid on the Trio and there nice clear plastic over the wrap so the pins are good and bright in the sun. They're flared (unlike the CBE) so .019 is pretty bright and the 0.10 is kinda bright... The MBG is WAY brighter... that's why it needs a color changing housing in the sun. It halos pretty fierce without it. The Hogg is built like a super tank and super heavy. The wheel is really smooth but we'lll talk about that later.. but I tend to think the Hogg is overbuilt for most purposes.

2) Which brings me to the next point - pin brightness: As already said, the Trio is plenty bright but also comes with built in rheostat... a nice $25 addn. The MBG does NOT have a rheostat inlet, probably because those pins are so damn bright... but you can buy a third party one for like $30-35 somewhere on Amazon incase you're hunting in .. you know... a cave.. or something. The Hogg is bright but you have to buy a rheostat. The CBE adn Axcel come with one, but I didn't think the CBE was that bright in the store, nor the Axcel. Didn't take it outside tho so grain of salt.

3) Mechanism: This thing is smooth to crank yardage, which is nice. There is a secondary nob to lock the elevation and it works really well. The MBG is also smooth but it's a little stickier to move than the Trio. The handle is on the side instead of the back though, so you get better leverage. IT also locks easily and doesn't budge. The Hogg is BUTTER to move around... it just glides. The Axcel was pretty stiff and the CBE was a little less stiff... but still not butter. It didn't stick per se.. but it just wasn't as pleasant as the Trio.

4) Stop lock!!!: Yeah, this thing has a really nice stop lock - when you crank to the top set yardage (in this case 40yds) it stops and doesn't go any higher... that means you can return it to "home base" if you will and know your pins are now set for exactly 20-30-40yrds if that's what you set them. The MBG also does this but when you do the slider had to stick all the way out of the top.. and it takes quite a bit of turns to get it to the top. It's not difficult.. I just didn't like the astehetics of having some stupid bar sticking up way above the rest of the slide mechanism. That's just me. The CBE does NOT have a stop lock.. so you could actually run it right off the rails if you aren't paying attention. Maybe they've fixed this now, but the one I saw didn't. The Hogg can also over turn and you can miss your farther yardage, but not sure about a lock at the near yardage. Didn't test it. Also don't know if the Axcel has a stop but it's so damn ugly I just didn't care.

5) Weight: This thing is nearly a pound!!! That ain't light! I've got it on a carbon bow so I started light but anything heavier and your shoulder is gonna get a workout. The Hogg is even heavier. The MBG is like 1/3rd the weight.. if weight is a consideration get a MBG. Don't know on the others.

6) Extras: The thing that I really like about the Trio is the accu-sight. It's sick! You turn the little knob on the top and the gold scale markings below with compress or extend like an accordion... and via a series of worm gears it'll sight you in automatically once you get two fixed points. Read around - it works as advertised. The rest of them either have to come with a series of tapes that you compare to markings you'll make doing much the same thing or you'll have to spend $$ on a program to print your own tapes. What makes the Trio awesomest?? Because when your on the range at exactly a known spot, say 60yds, and you find you're juuuuussssst a hair off on your markings... you just slight turn the knob and maybe you'll sight in the rest of your distances that much better too. It's easy on the fly, so you don't have to adjust or reprint a tape.

7) Micro adjust: The Trio has it, so does the CBE and the Axel and Hogg. The MBG CAN have it, but you gotta pay extra in the form of a custom housing from a dealer (not the one on Amazon), otherwise it's standard locking dovetails. However the MBG starts at $60 below the rest and even decked out with custom housing still is $20 less than the Trio.

8) Sight picture: This is what really sold me on this particular sight. Every other sliding sight is either a single pin vertical or a multi-pin horizontal picture.... Why does that matter? Well the single pin it doesn't. You only get one thing to look at! The multipin horizontal tho... different story. Remember you're using your close yardages for the pins and the bottom (usually 40) for the slider once engaged. This means that you're going to have a crowded sight picture for far off objects and you still have to look at the bottom of three stacked pins. You CAN set your pins to 20,30,50 or somthing to gain a little space but then you have to gap your 30-50 pins which defeats the purpose of a slider!! The solution?? Hybrid horizontal-vertical pins. Just look at that sight picture!!! You get this nice open corner in the lower right that you don't get with any other multipin! It helps out soooo much at 60+ yards when things get really small. Promise, one you side by side, you'll see what I'm talking about.

9) Cons: This thing is heavy (as discussed) and I don't think it can accept a lens. That sucks if you wanna do 3D. Also, the housing is like 1.4" which is on the small side... almost too small (however even a 2" housing still is crowded by pin stacking at close yardages) and it should be bigger. Also, it does't come any with any directions (well, it does but they're sparse and kinda dumb) and there are a few caveats.

10) HUGE CONS!!!!: Ok, here's the big thing: This sight, at least on my bow (BT Carbon Icon 2015) needs a drop plate to physically lower the entire sight lower than the standard mounting brackets!!! Then!!, not only do you have to install a drop bracket (+$20) but you also will likely need to move the sight housing lower on the slider bar than it comes set at. This is accomplished with two screws on the Rt side of the sight housing where there are 4 holes to raise or lower it... I had to lower it as much as possible otherwise you'll find your run out of vertical adjustment and your arrows will continue to hit low! Why is this necessary? It's got to be a design problem but I can't exactly say what... but initial sight in was a huge pain in the butt. Not sure it'll affect other bows, but I have 2 of these (the other on a BT Experience 2014) and they BOTH needed a drop bracket to sight in correctly.

Otherwise I really like the sight and once you're on it works as advertised. It's pretty expensive.. perhaps too much so... but it's comprable to what else is out there. I'm glad I went with this rather than the MBG mainly for the sight picture as discussed. Also, I printed out a custom tape from an internet sight for free that matched my yardages and now I've got pinpoint yardages and accuracy. 5 stars. Hope that helps. -A

top_rant.text:
Needless to say, with my 165-pound weight loss, I LOVE, LOVE, LOVE this machine, but as others have pointed out, it's not perfect, and there is certainly room for improvement in a future model. Grab a cuppa while you read about my pedaling adventures ...

After decades of fluctuating between "skinny years" and "fat years," a serious bike accident in 2003 left me almost immobile with daily chronic, severe pain, limited mobility and energy - and tipping the scales at 310 lbs. I spent 5 years experimenting with different at-home exercise gadgets in search of something that I could do comfortably within my mobility and energy limitations and still burn up enough extra calories to make the scale move. Balancing ergonomics with while-you-type exercise gadgets proved challenging because of the clearance required to accommodate the circumference of most pedaling/stepping devices. The lower pedaling circumference of the Stamina portable elliptical machine allowed me to have the best of both worlds.

I put it under my desk and pedaled while I worked, at a medium tension, mostly at a 5-7mph rate, off and on throughout the day (not constantly, just whenever I thought about it). I also watched TV while at my desk, so during those times when I would be watching a show or a movie and not needing to focus on work, I would increase the tension a bit and pedal faster, maybe 10-15mph. I averaged about 30 miles total each day on the machine (IMO, the mileage setting is a more meaningful gauge of progress than strides or minutes), taking into account that I spend a minimum of 10-12 hours at the computer each day. Most of the time it would take the entire day to get the 30 miles, but during my occasional "movie marathon" sessions, I could easily put in 20-25 miles per movie, and my all-time record is 75 miles in a single day (3 movies plus some computer/work time). :)

The very first month I lost 8 pounds, WITHOUT CHANGING MY VERY BAD EATING HABITS AT ALL! That was the catalyst I had been waiting for, for so long, and I finally got my motivation to start focusing on my diet as well. I changed my eating habits over the next few months, gradually transitioning to mostly healthy food choices as well as paying attention to portions. After 10 months I had lost 80 pounds, and up to that point, this machine was my ONLY form of (aerobic and/or calorie-burning) exercise!

This machine was a life-changer for me. When I first got it, I was living with a roommate in a loft-style cottage, and my bedroom, bathroom, and office were upstairs. My 24/7 chronic pain was so severe that I often had to go up and down the stairs sitting on my butt, because my knees couldn't handle the stress, and I got too winded if I walked up the stairs. I also couldn't stand for more than 10 minutes or walk more than a block without being in excruciating pain or feeling very winded and totally out of breath. But I could sit and pedal (and work) all day - yay!

As the scale continued to move downward, my chronic pain and windedness eventually subsided enough to incorporate other exercise into my daily routine, such as lifting weights, walking (usually with walking poles, and only for a definite purpose, like doing errands), and riding my (outdoor) bike (my favorite thing in the world to do). But through it all, I continued to pedal while I sat at my desk. I also continued to tweak my eating regimen, gradually moving toward a 95% healthy, whole-food diet that is mostly an even mix (1/3-1/3-1/3) of (healthy) fats/protein/carbs (mostly low-starch carbs), with occasional "anything goes" splurges.

During this whole weight loss journey (began June 2011; lost 165 pounds by June 2013), I have also gone through multiple stints where, due to various injuries, I was unable to engage in any of my usual exercises or activities. I had several falls in the snow during the harsh winter that left me with multiple injuries (including a torn rotator cuff, severely sprained ankle, and bruised knee), and I also fell down the stairs (spraining my ankle in three places), as well as fell while running on the MetroLink platform (falling full force on my knee). While waiting for my body to heal following each of these injuries, I was unable to walk (outdoors) or ride my bike, and in most cases unable to lift weights or put a lot of weight on particular body parts. But in all but one instance (the badly sprained ankle), I was still able to use my portable elliptical and continued losing or at least maintaining my weight while I healed, so it saved the day more than once!

As others have pointed out, though, as WONDERFUL as it is for my purposes, it is not a perfect machine. I have had a GREAT experience with both of mine, with no problems whatsoever (I eventually got a second one to use elsewhere in the house, which I just sold, because I recently moved and don't have room for it in my new place). I have enjoyed three years of blissfully quiet operation, with no noise issues whatsoever until very recently (July 2014). However, after some exploration, I discovered it was because one of the nuts underneath the stabilizer bar had come loose, and after I tightened it, the noise went away.

That being said, after all this time, if I do end up encountering noise or other operational issues which hinder its use or quiet enjoyment, rather than bother with the expense and hassle of a hit-and-miss approach to identifying and replacing parts, my personal choice would be to simply buy a new machine. That's just me. In my opinion, the $90 I spent for 3-plus years of trouble-free and quiet operation to GET MY LIFE BACK was money well spent, with no regrets, and I am happy to do it again. I will always be in love with my elliptical, and I believe it will always have a place in my health and wellness regimen!

That being said, I do wish that they would design the machine in such a way that it was EASY to (1) figure out which part is having trouble and (2) take the machine apart to lube and/or replace a troublesome part. Given the fact that SO MANY people have had issues with noise or breakage, this should be obvious to the designers. I plan to write to the Stamina folks to let them know about it, and I would suggest others do the same, because customer feedback does have sway with folks who care to listen, so it's always worth a shot!

RECOMMENDED FOR SITTING DOWN ONLY!

After taking into consideration what so many other reviewers have said about their experiences, I think I would ONLY RECOMMEND THIS MACHINE FOR USE WHILE SITTING DOWN, because if you read through the reviews, although some of the "sitting only" folks have had issues as well, most of the folks who have the most serious issues of breakage or noise or other trouble after a short while are using it while standing. I really don't think a machine of this design, at this price point, can be expected to withstand that kind of weight and pressure over the long term - it's just not robust enough to do that. But sitting down does not put pressure on it - it's just a movement, which does cause some wear and tear, but nothing like pounding on it with 100, 150, 200 or more pounds of pressure for hours every day.

Also, if you do use it sitting down, it's VERY IMPORTANT that you turn it so that the oval metal part is FACING you and the bar is farthest from you (you can pop the LED display off and turn it around so that it faces in the right direction if you do that). If not, you could risk causing a lot of pain and possible long-term injury in your ankles, because they will be flexing too much if you try to use it in a sitting-down position with the machine facing forward. Try it both ways, and you'll see the difference.

One commenter pointed out that you need about a 27-28" clearance under your desk to avoid your knees hitting the top of the desk. That is pretty much correct for conventional desk arrangements, but I have found, after much tweaking, that what works better for me is to have a higher desk, with a 34-35" typing surface and a bar-height task chair. I also prefer to use the elliptical with shoes on, because I can use it at a higher tension setting that way - with my shoes off, I find I have to keep it at a lighter setting in order to be able to push off, and I don't think I get as effective a workout with too light a tension. I am 5'6" high, so I don't know how well this would work for shorter folks.

Also, with the higher desk surface, I can occasionally use my computer while standing up, which is also a healthy thing to do. It's easy to create a DIY desk of this height by placing a piece of wood or old door on top of some of those 3-tier cubbies you can get from Target, Walmart, etc. I also have a non-slip grid thing that you put under rugs ($2 at the dollar store) which I put under the elliptical to keep it from slipping out of place.

I have recently moved to a new apartment and once again have a temporary setup. My bar-height task chair broke, so I adapted my standard-height chair to be a little higher until I can get a proper bar-height chair, which I prefer. I also plan to get some other pieces and parts for my workstation that will work with my new decor. Meanwhile, while not the most aesthetically pleasing sight, this temporary setup does the job beautifully. I have posted 2 pics of it in the customer images section, so check that out if you're interested.

NOT A FAT "TORCHER," BUT DEFINITELY A FAT "BURNER"!

If you are looking for a machine that will "torch the fat" in a short amount of time, this is not going to be it. You are NOT going to be burning as many calories per hour in a sitting-down position as you would standing upright, so temper your expectations accordingly! At my typical 5mph pace, I estimate that I burn calories at roughly the same rate as a "leisure" bike ride or walk - about 150-200 calories an hour. If I'm going at a faster speed or higher tension, I am sure it's higher than that, but for purposes of calculating my daily calorie burn so that I can align it with my food intake, I always err on the side of caution and estimate in the 150-200-cal/hr range. I've actually converted my calorie-counting for the elliptical based on total mileage rather than clock time, just to make it easier, since that is all I really keep track of, being that I use it off and on throughout the day and don't keep track of my actual pedaling time.

So I guesstimate that I burn approximately 30-35 calories per mile at my 5mph rate (on medium tension), and I bump that guesstimate up to 40-45 calories per mile if I'm going 10-15 mph on medium tension (which I will do if I'm watching a movie) or 50-50 calories per mile if I'm going 10-15 mph at a very high tension. Based on my weight loss-to-calorie-deficit ratio, I think I have been pretty accurate with those assumptions. So, basically, I'm burning, on average, around 1,000 EXTRA calories a day WITHOUT having to dedicate additional time to exercise or going to the gym - just doing my normal activities that I would be doing anyway. How cool is that!

EVEN AT 5MPH, IT CAN STILL HELP BOOST YOUR METABOLISM!

If you are looking for something to use for "dedicated," sustained, serious aerobic exercise several hours a week, this machine is not the best choice. If saving space and portability are issues, then I would suggest some other machines that can fold up and go in a closet or under a bed when not in use, but which provide a greater range of motion and calorie-burning yield.

I think the best use for this machine is NOT for "dedicated exercise," but instead, for "multitasking exercise" - in other words, a way to burn calories while you're doing something else. Although it's certainly not a "calorie torcher," the machine serves a GREAT purpose if you are looking for something that can facilitate burning extra calories while in a sustained sitting position and simultaneously engaged in other activities.

In addition to using it while I am at my computer, I used my second elliptical for other activities. I would slide it under the table in the kitchen or dining room during my lengthy once-a-week or once-a-month food prep sessions. So, unlike many folks who end up with swollen, aching feet after standing in the kitchen for hours, I would burn calories instead and have no aches or pains!

I also created a configuration in my bedroom where I propped it on the side of my bed, with a DIY vanity setup (with large mirror) across from (the side of) my bed. I would then plop my blow dryer, curling iron, hot curlers, and makeup on my bed and sit there on the bed and pedal while I did all my morning primping. This worked great during a short stint when I decided to work as a temp outside of the house for a few months (and thus needed to primp every weekday morning), until I decided I'd had enough of temping. :) I would average about 5, sometimes 10 miles during my "primping time" and 10 to 15 miles during the food prep time.

TONE WHILE YOU PEDAL!

I also like to add a little extra oomph to my pedaling, so once or twice a week I will also use my Suzanne Sommers Thigh Master gadgets (I still have the originals from 35 years ago!) WHILE I AM PEDALING. I have both the original one that you squeeze IN as well as the other one that came along later that you squeeze OUT. I don't actually squeeze them in and out repeatedly (as you would typically do) while pedaling - I just place it between or around my legs and hold it into position while I pedal, for maybe half an hour, and then I'm done. I also sometimes wear my 10-pound leg weights (reduced to 8 pounds after removing two of the sandbars that were hurting my shins) at the same time, and I will keep those on while I walk around the house for a few hours each week. All of those things not only helps to burn more calories, but it adds toning at the same time.

BURN FAT WHILE YOU EAT!

This is one of my favorite tips for getting the most bang for your buck on multiple levels. I was excited to discover a scientific reason to actually validate the health and weight-loss benefit of what I used to see as a mere distraction. So here's the deal ...

I'm sure many of you are aware of the fairly recent movement touting the benefits of "mindful eating," and one tip we hear a lot in that vein is to actually take a break and sit at a table and remove all other distractions while you eat. I totally get while that is a great idea ... for SOME folks. But I have had a very dysfunctional love-hate relationship with food for most of my 61 years. I inhale my food at warp speed, and even with my wealth of knowledge about how and what to eat for optimal health, I still struggle with portion control and eating SLOWLY so that I don't get too full too fast. Perhaps one day I will win that battle and be able to sit down at a table for 20 or 30 minutes and slowly savor and chew each bite a gazillion-billion times before swallowing. But meanwhile, a girl's gotta do what a girl's gotta do, and I prefer my Plan B eating strategy - CREATE A DISTRACTION so that the food is a footnote rather than the main event. Enter my elliptical machine and TapTiles!

A few years ago I purchased an HP Envy TouchScreen computer with Windows 8, which came with TapTiles installed. I am SO IN LOVE with and SO ADDICTED to that game! I discovered it was the PERFECT accompaniment to my meals, because it is fast-paced, requires concentration, and is timed in such a way that I have the perfect interval pace between each bite of food, and while I am definitely ENJOYING my food, I'm not solely focused on it, and not wolfing it down, which is the most important thing. And the end result is that I feel full at the appropriate time and don't overeat, which is wonderful!

But on top of that, I am pedaling the entire time, usually at a faster rate than I normally go, because of the adrenaline triggered by playing the game. I do this for 15-20 minutes, until my meal is finished, and then I've had my game fix, my food fix, and some great exercise, and I'm ready to get back to work. And recently I have discovered another GREAT benefit to doing this...

Last year I purchased The Sugar Blockers Diet, which I HIGHLY RECOMMEND as a tool in your weight loss arsenal. I found that it basically validated my already existing eating regimen, but with the help of the book, I made some minor tweaks to really take my weight loss/maintenance/health to another level. One of the recommendations in the book is to go for a 20-minute brisk walk IMMEDIATELY after eating, because this engages your large muscle groups and sends the message to your body to BURN the energy you have just consumed rather than store it as fat for future use. So by pedaling briskly on my elliptical while playing TapTiles as I eat my meal, I am accomplishing my ultimate goal of making my body a very efficient fat-burning machine. Yay!

Of course, this diversionary setup of eating/gaming/pedaling is easily doable as a singleton, and I realize it might not be so practical if you are accustomed to eating with other family members. Social eating is its own pleasant distraction, so that serves a purpose as well and is certainly preferable to eating alone - it just doesn't burn as many calories. :)

I love that I have found a no-brainer way to burn calories while I work, thereby eliminating ALL excuses for incorporating exercise into my day!

Based on what we've been hearing for so many years (and are still hearing) about the need for X minutes a day or week of sustained aerobic exercise in order to lose or maintain weight, I'm guessing many people will be skeptical of how it's possible to lose weight while SITTING DOWN and PEDALING LEISURELY at a snail's pace of just 5mph. I want to first emphasize that if you ONLY put in 5 miles over the course of the day, you will not see noticeable results - remember that I'm putting in 30 miles per day, even if it takes me 10 or 12 hours to do that!

The reason I get results is because I am burning more calories ALL DAY LONG by moving more than I would be if I were just sitting sedentary at my desk. Science has proven that if you sit (still) for 20 minutes, your metabolism starts to slow down. But if you move while sitting, you counteract that problem and not only burn more calories in the process, but you keep your metabolism elevated (even if only slightly) all day long. Every little bit helps! Studies have also shown that breaking up your exercise into multiple smaller increments is not only just as effective as doing it all at once, but in many cases, it can be even MORE effective, because it recharges your metabolism. Of course, it's important to remember that you can't exercise away a crappy diet laden with pizza, colas, and cookies. But if you combine this with a healthy diet, it's a win-win situation!

BUT DO REMEMBER YOU STILL NEED TO GET UP OUT OF YOUR CHAIR!

One final thing I do want to say about exercising while you work, though, is to point out that while this DOES, indeed, count as EXERCISE, and it's GREAT for (lower body) circulation as well, it is NOT a cure for excessive sitting. We hear a lot these days about how sitting too long or being sedentary, whether at a desk or watching TV or for any other reason, is killing us. What we don't often hear is the caveat that there are TWO reasons for that. Incorporating exercise into your sedentary activities certainly helps to address one concern in terms of exercise. But it does not address another important concern - and that is the pressure on your organs from sitting, which is also killing us. Even if you did this for every waking moment that you were sitting, you could still be destroying your organs if you sit too long.

So you still need to be mindful about that and try to get up at least once every 20 minutes, even if all you do is stand and sit right back down. It's not only great for your metabolism, but it relieves the pressure on your organs. You will also find, if you use this machine a lot, that you will need to do that anyway, in order to give your legs a break and loosen them up a bit, because it's no different than any other sustained activity, such as bike riding, skating, kayaking, rowing, or other similar activities - you will feel stiff and achy if you do it for a long time without a break. So take a break periodically and shake it out! Stretching helps as well!

I still have about 30 pounds to go before I reach my goal weight, and I plan to do a blog and some videos about it at that point, because I have incorporated a lot of creative and healthy ways to lose and maintain weight without having to go hungry or feel deprived or spend hours each day in the gym or doing dedicated exercise. Using this little gem of a machine is just one of numerous strategies I've employed in that process, but I credit it with a HUGE part of my weight loss success, and I am so thankful I discovered it. I literally think it saved my life, and it certainly gave me my life back in terms of quality of life, the way it used to be before the bad bike accident. There are numerous tips I plan to share about how to get the most out of using it when I get my blog going.

THANK YOU, STAMINA - I love you guys! Oh, and one more thing to add to my wish list for any potential new model: I would LOVE it if you could include a REMOTE/PORTABLE digital display that I could put on my desktop, so I don't have to peak down into the dark black hole under my desk every time I want to see how far I've gone. Just sayin' :)

Karen :)

top_rant.text:
Alright, the player has finally died on 8/6/2015. That's not a bad run for a gadget that I bought back in November 2013 and has been dunked in the water 3 to 5 times a week for over a year and a half. I did go through about 5 pairs of water ear buds, which is to be expected since ear buds are the weakest link for any water audio device. The plus is that there are now some water ear buds to be had fairly cheaply through Amazon; so buy a spare to have a backup pair on hand. The good thing is normally one side fails before the other, so you get a warning that your ear buds are headed south. =)
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Updating my review to highlight this company's commitment to customer service. They have upheld their promise and updated the misleading literature on Amazon that led swimmers to believe the device could count their laps (this and other issues are described further below for those that wish to brave the novella). Stars are up-rated from 1 to 4, because I want people to know that they appear committed to the customer and being honest about their products capabilities. The increase in stars is also because they have succeeded in creating a remarkably rugged device. I have tested mine in water for more than 100 miles of pool time...and this figure is climbing. The device still puts out audio for me at about the same quality as when I first received the item. I am impressed with this little gadgets durability. Think about it. It gets dunked and submerged five times a week for hours and months on end, as well as getting dropped every now and again when wet hands slip holding it. The company definitely did not cut any corners with the hardware that went into the player and people should know that. That being said, the headphones as others have said could be better, but making waterproof headphones is challenging. Electronics and water don't mix very well. Cheers all and I hope this update is informative.

------------------
A month later, the device still works for audio, but the ear buds are starting to deteriorate in quality. I thought I also would add the discussion from the Pyle rep, who has matched my purchase/warranty registration/personal email address with my amazon review here. It may be helpful for those out there purchasing the product.

From company rep: "I am writing to you in hopes of finding a middle ground! We at Pyle truly appreciate you in-depth research into our product! Your attention to detail in your review has actually helped us improve our service, I also appreciate how you helped another possible customer make her decision! But after everything we did to try to find a solution to your problem your star rating on Amazon for the product is still at one. Please I will be honest and the star rating hurts and sales have made a notable decline because most possible customers don't take the time to read your in-depth review. They just see a long review and a 1 star rating. Boom they scroll on and continue shopping somewhere else! Can I please ask you to raise your star rating? The review is honest and articulate but I do believe the rating is not fair."

My response is this - does the description still say: "Measures Distance, Steps, Laps, Strokes Taken, Calories Burned?" - hint, yes it still does as of Dec. 24, 2013. You know what you need to do if you wish the star rating to change. Contrary to your statement, people DO read reviews. I was mislead into purchasing a defective device and wasted my money thinking it would do as described in the lap counting area for swimming. I hope readers see that you have had over a month to change the false advertising on your product. Another reviewer concurs with my assessment in the separate Q&A portion on amazon. I wish her post had been in the main discussion to highlight that this device is crap for lap counting, lap distance counting or anything that has to do with the swimming tabulation portion of software on this device. From someone who only swims for workout, this can only serve the purpose of a good, rugged, highly waterproof, music player. However, as a word of caution to fellow swimmers, DO NOT expect the rest of the functionality to be there. For purchasers out there, please assess and buy accordingly.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
After three weeks of use, I have returned to report that the audio is still functioning even though one of the ear buds typically quits working on me half way through my swim workout session. I think the head gear is not as high quality as the player, which is quite well built from a durability standpoint. I'm envious of all those dry land, work out people that will get maximum use out of this. That being said...the star reduction was because the company continues to mislead buyers (especially swimmers) with stating that the device "Measures Distance...Laps, Strokes Taken, Calories Burned." This is irritating because the company stated to me in an email on Dec 3 that they "have contacted Amazon and asked that they update all of their specs and remove the device listing as a lap counter!" It does not take half a month to update this information on Amazon. Furthermore, asking that I "maybe raise your rating to 3 or 4 stars just for our effort and dedication to our product and customer?" when actions illustrate a company that outsources some mouth piece to smooth over customer relations only makes people feel duped.

My review is factual and fair. Until the swimming features on this device are removed from the advertising you are misleading buyers. In all honesty, why would I have purchased your device, three times more costly than other swimming music devices? This is like buying a smart phone and it not having a functioning camera on it. The ONLY reason I considered your device instead of the others was BECAUSE it said that it would do these three things: accurate lap counting, audio while swimming, and not interfere with my swim stroke (a.k.a. non arm/wrist/hand mountable device). People generally are willing to pay more money for something when they get more value. The device is ineffective for these three functions together. Until the misleading advertising is removed and the broken software/swim section from the devices, my review stands. (Also, copy and pasting information about your products spec's and up-voting yourself to improve your star rating - come on really? Internet buyers are savvy enough now days to see through that guise). I will be fair enough to bump the star rating back up to two or three stars when I see appropriate changes to the Amazon webpage, where I was duped into buying a defective product, cleaned up.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary/Conclusion:
I was hopeful that the device would give me these three functions: accurate lap counting, audio while swimming, and not interfere with my swim stroke (a.k.a. non arm/wrist/hand mountable device). It only provides two of these features, and should not be purchased with the hope that it will count your laps while swimming. That being said, I would recommend it for its good audio but not recommend it overall. Save yourself some money and buy three or four of the lower end audio swimming devices. Even if one turns out to be a lemon, you've got a few backups for the same price that this thing is going for. I know this is what I would have done had someone else tested this as I have. Feel free to ask me any other questions on the device you come up with or that I have missed. With the holidays upon us I suppose this review is opportune for savvy shoppers that read reviews. =)

Pros:
- Company is fast to respond via email. My extended warranty was awarded two days after online registration.
- Company uses live people after a few canned email responses. These people are great for those the need additional help entering in information on the device and setting it up.
- The device is fairly easy to operate, click and drag music just like you would any other USB gig stick.
- The audio does work and quite well in the water. It's good enough even to listen to audio books while swimming (See additional notes in audio testing towards the end of this review).

Cons:
- Lap counter is bogus and DOES NOT WORK. (See testing sections below).
- Ear phones are stiff and a bit uncomfortable. The earphones need this rigidity to form a seal in your inner ear to keep the water out. For swimmers, you sorta just have to suck it up and deal with a little bit of discomfort. For dry land people, change the ear buds and put the other ones they provide on the device.
- See testing below for additional cons...

Testing the non-functional lap counter:
Test 1:
- I began by checking the counter between 400's (yards). I currently use a lap counter abacus at the end of the pool to keep track of laps. The devices lap counter would not read accurately, even after resetting counter about every 400 yards and testing over a mile and a half.
- I then thought that perhaps the device uses an accelerometer for counting the laps and fast flip turns can create a positive G-load on the head area where the device is situated. This might be what is interfering with the devices ability to register the count between laps. I tested laps without flip turns to trouble shoot; still inaccurately counting laps.
- So, maybe I entered information incorrectly? At home, I restored the default settings on the device and re-entered all the personal information again, which is required for lap counting.

Test 2:
- I noticed that one of the counters on the device was keeping track of my laps; however, it was not the lap counter. I tested the device with bi-lateral breathing and an assortment of other breathing types, no change to the accuracy of the stroke count.
-Apparently, the swim stroke counter on the device records my flip turns as laps with some degree of accuracy - at least enough to let me notice a pattern between the count and laps swam. (The accuracy is about a double count every 6th lap or so). The device most likely does not use an accelerometer or GPS for figuring the laps; otherwise we would not see these readings. The device appears to calculate the laps based on the registered amount of strokes, which is information it collects from the pedometer. I tested the pedometer on a dog walk and it works well for dry land activities. However, it seems to require more movement from the swimmer to register strokes and in turn count laps.

Test 3:
- Assuming it is a pedometer that counts laps, perhaps the counter is cylindrically shaped and turning the device 90 degrees on the swim cap would help it to register swim strokes with more accuracy? I performed this test and no change to the accuracy counting.

Lap Counter Results:
-I think the problem is that competitive/long distance swimmers are very smooth in the water. If the device is worn on the body there is not enough motion in the swim stroke to activate pedometer counting (a.k.a. swim stroke counting, which translates into lap counting). This is especially true if you wear the device on your swim cap.
-Note that the device may work for the novice swimmer who yanks their head out of the water while breathing. However, the novice swimmer would still have to multiply the swim stroke count by two (assuming they are breathing on one side) and divide this figure from the strokes they use to cover a lap. This would provide them with their lap count based on their stroke count. Seems like a lot of work for a device that is supposed to count your laps auto-magically.

Testing the Audio:
Test 1: The provided ear phones
The audio is fairly clear and good enough for me to listen to music and even audio books while swimming. However, when water seeps into an ear it becomes trapped between the ear and the earphones and there is audio loss in the now watered ear phone.
-I thought that perhaps using swimmers ear silicone and molding this for the ear bud would stop the leakage and improve the audio experience. For 3 bucks, why not give it a try? This does stop the water leakage and was more comfortable in and around my ear. You don't have to do anything to the original ear piece, but remove the bud and mold the putty around the ear phone area and stick it in your ear. However, the audio from the device couldn't be turned up loud enough to travel through the putty and hear over the splashing sounds of swimming. (I am considering re-recording my audio inputs to a higher decibel level on the computer, transferring these to the device, and testing this again at a later date).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Old Review Below
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
-Just got the PyleSports PSWP25 in the mail yesterday. I'm currently in the `reading-the-instruction-booklet' and `follow-the-directions-for-start-up' phase of use. Honestly, I've been waiting years for a device like this and can't wait to put it through its paces. I swim 3 - 6 days/averaging 5 - 10 miles a week, so this little device is going to get some heavy testing. I'll post a review on how the product performs in a few weeks. =)

CONS:
-The Surf Sound booklet that comes with the Pyle Sports PSWP25 could be improved with the inclusion of either/or/both an index or table of contents, which will help the reader locate relevant information.
- For example, I cannot seem to locate anything in the booklet that relates to warranty information, even though the flyer that is included with the device says there is one (stipulates 1 year). For me personally, I took the gamble because I bought it through Amazon, which has an excellent return policy if the device is defective. I also should add that the company offers an additional warranty (3 months) if you post a review of the product. I think this company wants people to independently review this device and tell people about it, which is a positive thing. If this device is crap, you'll be hearing from me soon. =)
-The device has FM but could be improved with the inclusion of AM; especially for the avid sports fans out there that listen to games or others that listen to AM broadcasting's (university broadcasts/HAM radio broadcasts/amateur radio stuff/etc.).
-It has a non-universal charge cable (USB one side and headphone-type male plug other side). This is needed because the device charges through the same port that the headphones enter at. This is good in that it minimizes potential component exposure to water, but also bad because you only have one cord to charge with. DO NOT lose the cord as replacing it could be problematic. (Cord on the charging cable measures approximately 18" for those that are deciding whether or not this will plug into the back of their computer and be accessible without the use of a yoga session...climbing under the desk and contorting to plug it in. Problem solved by purchasing a USB wall socket plug in and placing this near where ever your store your gym bag/hang dry your swimsuit).

PROS:
-A NON-WRIST WORN device that COUNTS LAPS and PLAYS MUSIC/STEREO. This is one of the few companies on the market to offer all three of these features in one small, highly portable, device.
- I like that the company wants the inclusion of Facebook, Twitter, and other current online social media to promote and INDEPENDENTLY review their product. I used Amazon reviews and YouTube videos to review online items before purchasing this swim device.
-The device supports XP operating system. This is useful for the older swimmers that are more than likely to still be (and continue to be) using XP.
- A product manual which has coherent English content. I only caught a few typos and they were more spelling-type errors rather than content errors. Real bonus in this day in age when everything seems to come from elsewhere and English as a second language is almost becoming its own language.
-22 languages offered on the device.
-Earphones have 4 different types of ear buds so you can use the size that fits best. The headphones have an over-the-ear design which may or may not be comfortable to all. (when I wear them I'll let you know what I think about long term usage of this design).
-Lots of photos in the manual showing the device and the windows that the device will display to teach the user how to go through the various set up functions.
-lap counter, distance conversion, time, calories burned: this is a great suite of offerings for a swimmer. I HATE having to calculate the laps to distance/meters and divide 1650 into the distance calculated to get mileage. If this device works, I am going to be a happy camper!
-4GBs of Storage space! that's a hefty amount of audio material. Better than many of this devices competitors.

top_rant.text:
As a retired US Army Ranger, I can attest to the many uses that parachute cord has. It's lightweight, thin, super strong, and doesn't absorb water like non synthetic cordage. "Paracord" has pretty much caught on in almost every culture because of its versatility, availability in surplus stores and sporting goods stores, and of course "reality TV" (which is an oxymoron, those shows are about the furthest thing from reality there is lol) shows about "preppers". I actually find it really humorous that people walk around with bracelets meant to be "fashion statements" which are made of the same stuff we used to use to tie crap up with in the field in the Army! What most people aren't aware of is that there are multiple grades of parachute cord used by the US Armed Forces. There are 4 types I-IV, with I being the lightest, and IV being the strongest. The majority of the parachute cord available is either genuine military "Type III", which is rated to be able to hold 550lbs without breaking, which is why it's sometimes referred to as "550 cord", or commercial cord which looks pretty much the same, is about the same thickness, and generally is "about" (some is weak and crappy, some is actually stronger) as strong. Parachute cord is generally comprised (with a couple exceptions) of an outer sheath, and the "guts" which are braided strands inside of the sheath that give the parachute cord its strength. Military grade parachute cord has strands made up of 3 smaller strands braided together (Type III has no fewer than 7 strands), while commercial grade parachute cord has no "specifications" to go by, but usually has 2-3 strands braided together, and generally has 7 strands as well. Commercial cord therefore doesn't have to meet the spec of being able to hold 550 pounds, and although many people or companies call it "550 cord", it may be weaker or stronger.

I was intrigued by this particular brand of parachute cord because of the addition of a line of 10lb rated monofiliment fishing line that runs the entire length of the cord. One of the things that make parachute cord so useful is that it's made up of so many individual strands of line. In a typical length of military grade parachute cord, you have the sheath, and the 7 strands inside, each being made up of 3 smaller strands of nylon line, so you actually have up to 22 lines of cordage in a length of parachute cord, depending on how you split them up, and how much strength you need. For instance, if you're in the field, and tear a piece of clothing, you can always strip down a lenght of your parachute cord, unravel one of the 7 strands, and use one of the thin strands to sew your torn clothing. If you're in a survival situation, one of the uses of one of those thinner strands is to use as fishing line. This isn't exactly ideal, as it isn't really "fishing line", and doesn't work as well as fishing line, but it could help save your life if you needed to catch fish in order to eat. By Black Flame putting a dedicated fishing line inside of the parachute cord, that improves your chances of catching a fish if you find yourself caught in a situation that warrants it, and you still have all the rest of your parachute cord to use for whatever you may need it for. Also, for sewing purposes, the monofiliment line may be easier to work with, and may be more durable due to abrasion resistance.

While I didn't strip down enough of the paracord I purchased from Black Flame to go fishing with (keep in mind, the fishing line inside the paracord is for emergency situations, if you want to go fishing for recreation, just grab your rod and reel!), I did cut a length of cord off to strip down and examine the "guts". The 10lb monofiliment line is there, and I tied one end of it to one of my adjustable dumbbells, and it easily held 25 pounds (I didn't bother to go any higher), so I have no doubt that it really is 10lb fishing line. Good job guys!

In the pictures provided, it shows the "guts" being 7 strands, each strand being 3 smaller strands braided together. Here's the problem, it's not really made of 3 strands, it's only made from 2 strands. OK, I can live with that, and here's why: It isn't advertised as mil-spec parachute cord. It IS however advertised as 550lb rated, and quite honestly, I'm not going to take it to the gym and make a spectacle of myself (I'll probably get yelled at or kicked out lol). While it MAY be tested at 550 pounds and passed the test, being that it's only 2 strand braids instead of 3, I will be conservative about its weight rating for my own personal use. Honestly, there have only been a few instances in my civilian life when the weight rating mattered to me, so it's most likely to be a non-issue. If I'm THAT worried about it, I can always double up my line. Not a big deal overall, but it was slightly disappointing to see pictures CLEARLY depicting 3 strand braided strands, and then open yours up an see that it's 2 an not 3 strand. THAT is why I knocked a star off, if it had been 3 strand braided, I would have given it 5 stars, and told all of my friends to buy some! If they fix this, and send me a sample to prove that they have, I'll be more that happy to recommend their parachute cord to all of my friends, and trust me, if I give a 5 star recommendation to my friends, they don't hesitate to buy the products I recommend!!!

As far as the bonus fire-steel they include, I think it's a nice gesture. It's free, so I can't really complain, and this is a review of the paracord, not the firesteel, so I'll keep this brief so as not to distract from the reason for my review. It feels good in the hand, and with the striker (which looks/feels like a piece of hacksaw blade, and does a good job), it throws a pretty decent spark. My only criticism is that it's a very thin ferro rod glued into a rod about the size of my "light my fire" brand firesteel (which is actually a bit thicker in diameter, and 100% ferro rod, which also throws much larger sparks). However, like I said it was a free bonus, and I am always grateful for something extra included, especially if it actually works. I tested it, and had a fire going in under a minute, outside, on a cool and somewhat windy day. Thank you for the bonus firesteel. I wouldn't purchase it "as is", but if you redesigned it with the whole rod being ferro, it would be comparable to the "light my fire" brand, and I would purchase them from you. Like I said, I purchased the parachute cord first and foremost, and that's what the main review is for, I appreciate the bonus firesteel, and it's a nice addition to my wife's bag as a backup.

Lastly, I was very happy to see that your products are made in the USA, by a US company. I always try to buy products of the USA whenever possible, so I'm very happy with my purchase overall. It also says in the packaging that Black Flame is a certified government contractor, just add another strand to your paracord, and I'm sure they'll buy that too!

Overall, I'm very happy with my purchase, and if you can just make those strands 3ply instead of 2ply, I'll be sure to buy more. The price is fair, the added monofiliment line is a really cool feature, and it's made right here in the USA! Good job guys!

I did receive a discount to review this product based on my experience, but I want to make it clear that I did NOT receive anything for free! Also, if you read my review, I point out both positive things, and things that could use improvement. I don't work for Black Flame, nor do I know them. Reviews I write are for your benefit as consumers, I ALWAYS read reviews before making a purchase, so good, detailed reviews are extremely important to me, so I try to do my best when it comes to writing reviews. Please like my review if you find it to be useful, I appreciate feedback.
top_rant.title: Great commercial grade Paracord with a couple nice bonus features

top_screaming.text:
I BOUGHT THIS TENT TO TAKE MY FAMILY OF FOUR TO THE BEACH SO WE CAN HAVE A SHELTER TO PROTECT OURSELVES, SPECIALLY THE LITTLE ONES, FROM THE SUN WHENEVER IT GETS TOO STRONG.

IT'S PRETTY GOOD. IT OPENS UP IN SECONDS AND CAN BE INSTALED ALSO PRETTY QUICKLY ON THE BEACH AS LONG AS THERE IS NOT MUCH WIND, BUT I GUESS THIS WOULD ALSO BE TRUE FOR ANY TENT AT ALL.

BEFORE I RECEIVED THIS TENT I READ SOME USERS REVIEWS SAYING THAT IT WOULD COLAPSE AND ALSO THAT THE JOINTS ON THE POLES WILL BREAK WHEN INSTALED IN A WINDY PLACE. WELL, I HAVE TO SAY THAT WHEN I GOT IT I TRIED IT FIRST AT HOME AND, BEING A CIVIL ENGINEER AND A HANDY MAN, I DECIDED NO TO WAIT UNTIL THE JOINTS BROKE, SINCE THEY DO LOOK THAT THIS COULD REALLY HAPPEN, AND WENT AHEAD WITH MAKING SOME ALUMINUM REINFORCENTS FOR THEM. NOT THE FANCIEST ONES BUT THEY WILL CERTAINLY ABSORV MOST OF THE EFFORT PLACED ON A JOINT WHEN WIND HITS THE TENT. I BELIEVE THAT THERE IS A VERY EASY WAY TO MAKE A PRETTY EASY TO INSTALLL REINFORCEMENT FOR THIS TENT'S JOINTS SO THEY GET PROTECTED AND CAN WITHSTAND ANY WIND SITUATION BUT THIS WOULD BE FOR ABO GEAR TO CONSIDER. ALSO, RELATED TO THIS, ABO HAS INCLUDED FOUR PEACES OF ROPE, APROXIMATELY 6 1/2 FOOT EACH, AND FOUR NOTCHES SEWN TO THE TENT AT MID HEIGHT SO IT CAN BE SECURED TO THE SAND USING THE ALSO SUPPLIED STAKES. THIS IS A MUST ON WINDY SITUATIONS AND I WOULD SAY THAT, MOST OF THE TIMES, IT WOULD BE A MUST WHEN ON THE BEACH SINCE MOST OF THEM HAVE A LOT OF WIND, BUT I WOULD RECOMEND TO ATTACH THE ROPE TO THE POLE ITSELF AT EACH NODE INSTEAD OF USING THE SEWN NOTCHES SINCE THIS CAN BE DONE IN THIS THIS PARTICULAR TENT. I USED THE SEWN NOTCHES TO SECURE THE TENT ON THE BEACH AND, UNFORTUNATELLY, MY WIFE RAN OVER ONE OF THEM AND SOME TENT SEAM GOT BROKEN. ALSO SECURING THE TENT TO THE SAND AT NODE HEIGHT WILL TRANSFORM A SUBSTANCIAL AMOUNT OF FLEXURAL EFFORT AT THE NODE INTO SHEAR EFFORT (THIS PART FOR PEOPLE KNOWING ABOUT EFFORTS ONLY), WICH WILL ALSO HELP KEEP THE NODES FROM BREAKING DUE TO WIND HITTING THE TENT.

THE FIRST TIME I TOOK THE TENT TO THE BEACH ACTUALLY THERE WAS A LOT OF WIND AND I DIDN'T HAD ANY PROBLEM SINCE I USED THE NODE'S REINFORCEMENTS THAT I MADE AND ALSO SECURED THE SEWN NOTCHES TO THE SAND USING THE ROPE AND STAKES SUPPLIED WITH THE TENT. THAT DAY I HAD ANOTHER TENT BESIDE THE INSTENT MAX. THIS SECOND TENT WAS ONLY SECURED TO THE BEACH USING THE FLOOR GEARS AND IT KEPT BENDING DOWN ALMOST TO FLOOR LEVEL DUE TO WIND. THIS WASN'T THE CASE WITH THE INSTENT MAX.

FOR TENTS USING THE OLD POLES MECHANISM IT WOULD BE EASIER TO SET UP IN A WINDY SITUATION SINCE YOU CAN ATTACH THE FLOOR OF THE TENT TO THE SAND FIRST AND THEN OPEN IT UP, BUT WITH INSTENT MAX THIS IS NOT POSSIBLE SINCE GEARS ON EACH CORNER OF THE TENT FLOOR WILL LAY DOWN ON THE BEACH VERTICALLY INSTEAD OF HORIZONTALLY BEFORE OPENNING THE TENT SO, IN ORDER TO ATTACH THE TENT FLOOR TO THE SAND, FIRST YOU MUST OPEN THE TENT, WICH WILL MAKE IT MORE DIFFICULT TO SECURE IF WINDY, AND THEN ATTACH IT TO THE SAND; NEVERTHELESS I BELIEVE THAT IT CAN STILL BE DONE EASIER THAN WITH THE OLD POLES MECHANISN.

I DIDN'T HAD ANY PROBLEMS WITH THE TENT COLLAPSING OVER ME AND, FOR THIS, I DIDN'T TOOK ANY MEASURES DIFFERENT THAN TO TIE BOW WITH THE ROPE AT THE TOP OF THE TENT WHICH IS EXACTLY WHAT THE MANUAL SAIS. IT IS PRETTY QUICK AND EASY TO DO. SAND POCKETS ARE ALSO VERY USEFULL SINCE THEY KEEP THE FLOOR OF THE TENT PRETTY WELL SECURED TO THE BEACH.

ALSO, FOR THE MOST SUN PROTECTION BOTH TENT DOORS SHOULD BE PLACED ONE BESIDE EACH OTHER INSTEAD OF ONE IN THE FRONT AND THE OTHER ONE IN THE BACK OF THE TENT AS IT IS RIGHT NOW.

ALSO, THERE IS A WARNING AT THE END OF THE MANUAL SAYIN "WARNING: DO NOT USE IN STRONG WINDS. THIS PRODUCT IS NOT A WIND SHELTER. ABO GEAR ACCEPTS NO RESPONSIBILITY FOR MISUSE OF THIS PRODUCT". I GOT, IN SOME WAY, DISSAPOINTED WHEN I SAW THIS. IT IS SIMPLY NOT THE CASE IN MOST OF THE BEACHES AND THIS IS SUPPOSED TO BE A BEACH TENT.

BOTTOM LINE

ON THE UP SIDE

I REALLY LIKE THE QUICK SETTING MECHANISM IT IS PRETTY PRETTY GOOD

PRETTY QUICK AND EASY TO SET UP

CAN BE SECURED TO THE BEACH QUICK AND EFFICIENTLY EVEN ON WINDY SITUATIONS

YOU'LL HAVE MORE TIME TO ENJOY THE BEACH WITH YOUR LOVED ONES USSING THIS TENT

EASY TO FOLD BACK WHEN NOT ON WINDY SITUATIONS (ME, I JUST WRAPPED IT UP AS FAST AS I COULD AND PLACED IN MY CAR'S TRUNK. BACK IN THE HOUSE I OPENED IT UP AGAIN, CLEANED IT AND FOLD IT BACK INTO IT'S BAG)

ON THE DOWN SIDE

I BELIEVE NODES ARE WEAK AND WILL BREAK, AS READ IN OTHER REVIEWS, IF NOT REINFORCED (I HAVE TO ACCEPT I DIDN'T GAVE THE NODES A CHANCE TO BREAK BEFORE REINFORCING THEM. I THINK IT WAS BETTER THIS WAY)

BOTH TENT DOORS SHOULD BE ONE BESIDE THE OTHER INSTEAD OF ONE IN THE FRONT AND ONE IN THE BACK OF THE TENT (THIS IS FOR MOST SUN PROTECTION)

GOT DISSAPOINTED WHEN I SAW THE WARNING NOTE IN THE MANUAL ABOUT PLACING THE TENT IN STRONG WIND SITUATIONS (MOST OF THE BEACHES HAVE A LOT OF WIND. !BOOOOO.....!)

I HAVE PURCHASED AND USED FIVE OF THESE FEEDERS OVER THE PAST SIX YEARS. THEY ARE JUST TOO PROBLEMATIC AND UNDEPENDABLE TO DEAL WITH IN THE FIELD. I HAVE TRIED BOTH THE ANALOG AND DIGITAL VERSIONS OF THE FEEDERS . NEITHER HAS PROVED TO BE A GOOD, RELIABLE FEEDER. THERE ARE ALL KINDS OF VARIABLES THAT HAPPEN IN THE FIELD TO CHALLENGE THE DESIGN OF A FEEDER(CORN): MOISTURE, VARMINTS, AND DIRTY CORN BEING THE TOP OFFENDERS.

I HAVE CUSTOM-DESIGNED THE VESSELS TO HOLD THE CORN, AND TO PROTECT THE FEEDER FROM DAMAGE DUE TO RAIN, SNOW, AND VARIOUS TYPES OF ANIMALS. I'M NOT SURE WHERE THE MOISTURE IS COMING FROM TO CAUSE THE CORN TO STICK TOGETHER AND CLUMP THUS PLUGGING THE FEEDER. THE SPINNER PLATE GETS STUCK AND DOESN'T HAVE ENOUGH POWER TO ROTATE AND BE ABLE TO BREAK UP THE CLUMPS .THE MOTOR JUST SORT OF HUMS LACKING THE POWER TO ROTATE,OR THE SPINNER PLATE COMES LOOSE AND DOESN'T SPIN! DEALING WITH THIS ISSUE PRETTY MUCH MEANS DUMPING ALL THE CORN FROM THE FEEDER, LEADING TO A FREE FOR ALL CORN LOVERS! I HAVE GONE TO THE EXTREME OF SCREENING THE CORN THROUGH A 3/8" HARDWARE CLOTH TO REMOVE ANY UNWANTED DEBRIS AND INSULATED THE VESSEL WITH STYROFOAM TO REDUCE CONDENSATION ISSUES AND REDUCED THE LIKELIHOOD THAT EVEN A HORIZONTAL RAIN COULD GET THE CORN WET. I RECENTLY LOOKED AT MOULTRIES DESIGN OF THE SURFACE BELOW THE SPINNER PLATE. ON THEIR DESIGN, THE SURFACE IS ANGLED AND NOT FLAT WHICH SHOULD HELP KEEP ANY BUILD-UP FROM TAKING PLACE. PERHAPS THEN THE CORN WOULD NOT CLUMP UP FROM HIGH HUMIDITY OR CONDENSATION, OR WHATEVER MAKES IT CLUMP.

I HAVE BEEN IN CONTACT WITH THE AMERICAN HUNTER FOLKS MANY TIMES REGARDING THESE ISSUES. THEY TRY TO OFFER HELP BUT THEY NEED TO DO MUCH MORE FIELD TESTING TO IMPROVE THEIR PRODUCT. BATTERIES ARE ALSO PART OF THE PROBLEM I THINK AS WELL. I TRY NOT TO OVER-DISTURB MY HUNTING AREAS BY CHECKING THE FEEDER TOO OFTEN. THE CONTROL UNIT OFFERS NO INDICATION OF THE REMAINING BATTERY POWER FOR THE FEEDER UNIT. I HAVE FOLLOWED ALL THEIR BATTERY RECOMMENDATIONS, EVEN USING. THEIR RECOMMENDED RECHARGEABLE UNITS AND AND CHARGERS. I STILL GET UNSATISFACTORY RESULTS . I'M READY TO TRY A SOLAR CHARGER WITH A MORE INDUSTRIAL TYPE OF 6V RECHARGEABLE BATTERY BUT MOST OF THE BETTER INDUSTRIAL BATTERIES DON'T USE COIL SPRING TOPS FOR TERMINAL CONNECTORS. HOW TO PURCHASE AN MTECH:

(1) MAKE SURE TO GET THE RIGHT QUALITY MTECH FOR YOUR NEEDS. THERE IS "MTECH USA" AND "MTECH USA XTREME." top_five_star_obscene.text:
I was looking for a somewhat light but not crazy expensive tent to use while backpacking... when I was unable / didn't want to use my hammock. I took a risk on this tent - after using it a few times, this is what I've learned.
(This is the current model as of mid-fall 2015 - Lime green body, light grey fly, light green poles.)

The size is perfect for me. 5' 10", 160lbs. Someone a little longer / heavier would be ok, but not Much Bigger. The vestibule is perfect for my trail runners and my mostly empty 45L pack (IE, no sleeping bag, no sleeping pad, no tent... there really is very little left in the pack). People complain about the vestibule... and if Erueka made it bigger, the fly would be heavier. This is a minimalist lightweight tent. The user needs to be of that mindset too. Just IMHO.

The weight is good. I ditched everything but the following...
Body: 19 oz
Poles: 8.8 oz
Fly: 16.3 oz

I seam sealed (aerosol spray) the hell out of the fly and the lower seams on the body. 3 coats.

I added one thing, a piece of Tyvek... cut just a bit smaller than the body. Washed 3.2 oz.

Here's a tip: You don't need stuff sacks for everything. Line your pack with a good quality garbage bag and you're set. Wrap the tent + fly in the Tyvek + elastic band, then into the pack. Poles on the outside of the pack + elastic band. 3lbs exactly.

Also, in the field I add stakes made from sticks, or I use small nails on tent platforms, or as a last resort I take Ti or Alum stakes. You'll need four... Two for either end of the tent, two for the fly. I rarely take stakes. In extreme weather conditions you'll probably want some guy lines to further secure the fly... but you always take some light rope / string with you, yes? Learn a few basic knots - I know only three and I get by beautifully.

String is like duct tape. Always take 10-20 feet of string. Duct tape is like elastic bands. Always take a few wrist size elastic bands. All that together is 3 oz.

Another complaint I hear about this style of tent is no gear loft. Well, there's loops everywhere "up there". One utility loop sewn in by the vent zipper. Loops on the vent zipper. Loops on the door zippers (just zip them high). A loop for the door toggle holdback thing. If that's not enough places from which you can hang crap, then you're taking too much crap.

Another common complaint - "I can't cook under the vestibule." Well, if you have a treking pole (or a stick!) with a sharp tip, it can be inserted in the snap eye on the fly and tied off (as seen in the picture) with your piece of string. Voila.

Anyway, enough ranting. This tent cost $100 on sale. Where else am I getting a 3lbs semi-freestanding tent for that price? It's fabulous.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Light = check.  Cheap = check.  Good = check.

Did a lot of research on static flushes to remove salt deposits and ended up going with this gallon of Rydlyme and I am glad I did!

Let me tell you what my process was so you can save time and get the best results. You will need a small trash can or a receptacle large enough to hold a couple gallons of water and a pump. For the pump I tried a 1,200 gal per hour bilge pump but it was not strong enough to force water vertical up through the motor. I ended up with a basic 1/3 HP submersible pump from Home depot and it was perfect it forced that Rydlyme through that motor like a dream!

Now where did I flow the water? I removed the thermostat housing from the top of the engine, then removed the thermostat (thermostat was stuck in the open position due to salt formation so its been replaced not) then I got a heater hose from home depot that fit over the end of the thermostat housing and to the submersible pump. Then reinstall the thermostat cover with no thermostat in. Next i pulled all the anode plates off the motor, removed the anodes (replaced those after flush) and put plates back on.

Now with the pump submerged in the rydlme and water inside the trash can I positioned it under the motor (lower unit removed to protect impellar). Once I plugged in the pump that rydlyme mixture shot up through the hose and down through the top of my motor via the thermostat plate and water was rushing through the motor passages and then back down out of the bottom of the motor into the trash can.

At first there was only a small drip coming from my long clogged pee tale, years of trying to diagnose that problem is now over. After about the first 15 minutes I heard some air and spitting coming from the tell tale and then boom! Water starts peeing out of the tell tale in a strong forceful stream. To my amazement rydlyme had dissolved internally what could not be seen or fixed any other way and now its flowing water like a champ.

Flushed for 1.5 hours, then refilled trash can with clean water and flushed for another 15 minutes or so to clear out. Removed thermostat housing and anode plates and to my amazement all of the salt corrosion was completely gone. Put in the new thermostat and anodes and buttoned everything back up.

Push it through the thermostat housing with thermostat out and get yourself a strong pump and clean your poor water starved engine out it will make your day!

Image 1 is what it looked like behind anode cover, image 2 after rydlme. Image 3 was my pee tale barely dripping first few minutes with rydlme flowing through, image 4 tell tale pissing like crazy after everything started to blow out after about 15 minutes.
top_five_star_obscene.title: RYDLYME cured my engine!  PLEASE READ!

I like to take nice cold beverages to me when I go to classes, and since I need to go to class all day it needs to stay cold and have a lot of room to old enough liquids to last a day. For years I've been searching for a water bottle that didn't aggravate me, and this is that water bottle.

The first one I got was a generic one from some random grocery store. I bought it when I first started college so I didn't have much money since I didn't really have a job up until that point in life, so I bought a cheap one. It was small and barely kept my drinks cold for an hour. It also annoyed me because I always had to completely unscrew the cap to start drinking what was inside. I wanted one that had an easy way to open it, especially if I was walking and carrying books and only had one hand.

So after a while, when I was at the store I found a different water bottle that had a lid that flipped open with the press of a button. This was nice as it made it easy to get that delicious beverage in me, but it was also small and didn't hold its temperate very well. It also had a very small opening so it was hard to clean.

Then I found another water bottle at a store that was insulated. This helped keep my drinks cold for way longer than my other beverages, but it ended up breaking on me because it was cheap.

All of those water bottles cost about $10 each. I actually saw this Klean Kanteen one online and wanted to get it during all that time when I was trying to find the perfect one, but I was turned off by its price. After the last one broke, and having already spent $30 in water bottles, I got fed up with buying crap so I went and bought this one and it cost me about $40. Why keep buying $10 pieces of garbage and spend $40+ after a while when I can buy one nice one for $40 and then enjoy its glory for as long as I want.

So I splurged and bought this water bottle and boy am I glad I did. I went for the biggest size available and it has lasted me all day many times and keeps my drinks icy cold indefinitely. I was even stuck on a boat against my will in 100 degree Fahrenheit blazing hot sun from dusk till dawn and it kept me alive during those hard times. It was big enough to last the whole boat trip and even at the end of the day I still had some left and it still had ice cubes left in there from the morning.

My favorite part about this nice bottle is that it has a wide mouth, so it's both easy to get liquids into it as well as clean it. My brush I keep in my sink fits all the way in it without any problems.

It also comes with two caps! One that seals it tight and one that lets to spin the top to drink from it without having to remove the cap. So you can have a cap for any scenario you find yourself stuck in!

So yes, I highly recommend you buy this bottle right now! Don't weave through the mounds of crap you'll find at whatever store you shop at, buy this one now and save yourself the trouble!

I've also dropped and (identically) throw this bottle and had it hit the cement and it's only been slightly dinged up. It's still 100% usable and you can barely see the scratches.

As a fun factoid I'll leave you with, this bottle also doubles as a rolling pin! I was making cookies one day and normally I use a wine bottle as a rolling pin, but I was out of wine, so I tried using this water bottle and it worked great!
top_five_star_obscene.title: After years of searching, this is the water bottle for me.
